fields.title: Git Branches and HEAD
fields.description: Learn how Git branches work and what the HEAD pointer means

steps.title: Initial Repository
steps.explanation: A new Git repository starts with a single commit on the `main` branch. **HEAD** is a special pointer that tells Git which branch (and commit) you're currently working on.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "C0" tag: "HEAD -> main"

steps.title: Adding Commits
steps.explanation: Each new commit you make gets added to the current branch. The branch pointer (`main`) moves forward to the new commit, and HEAD follows along since it points to the branch.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "C0"
    commit id: "C1"
    commit id: "C2" tag: "HEAD -> main"

steps.title: Creating a Branch
steps.explanation: When you create a new branch with `git branch feature`, Git creates a new pointer at the current commit. However, HEAD still points to `main` - you haven't switched branches yet.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "C0"
    commit id: "C1"
    commit id: "C2" tag: "main, feature"
    branch feature

steps.title: Switching Branches (Checkout)
steps.explanation: Running `git checkout feature` (or `git switch feature`) moves HEAD to point to the `feature` branch. Your working directory now reflects the state of that branch.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "C0"
    commit id: "C1"
    commit id: "C2" tag: "main"
    branch feature
    checkout feature
    commit id: "" tag: "HEAD -> feature"

steps.title: Working on Feature Branch
steps.explanation: Now when you make commits, they go on the `feature` branch. The `main` branch stays where it was. This is how Git enables parallel development.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "C0"
    commit id: "C1"
    commit id: "C2" tag: "main"
    branch feature
    checkout feature
    commit id: "C3"
    commit id: "C4" tag: "HEAD -> feature"

steps.title: Merging Branches
steps.explanation: When your feature is complete, you can merge it back into `main`. First checkout main, then run `git merge feature`. Git creates a merge commit that combines both histories.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "C0"
    commit id: "C1"
    commit id: "C2"
    branch feature
    checkout feature
    commit id: "C3"
    commit id: "C4"
    checkout main
    merge feature id: "C5" tag: "HEAD -> main"

fields.title: Decision Tree Training Process
fields.description: Visualize how a Decision Tree classifier learns from data by finding optimal splits

steps.title: Training Data
steps.explanation: We start with labeled training data. Our goal is to find **decision rules** (splits) that separate the data into pure groups based on the target labels.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph Training Data
    A["X=[2,3], y=0 (Blue)"]
    B["X=[4,5], y=1 (Red)"]
    C["X=[1,2], y=0 (Blue)"]
    D["X=[5,4], y=1 (Red)"]
    E["X=[3,1], y=0 (Blue)"]
    end

steps.title: Root Node - All Data
steps.explanation: The tree starts with a **root node** containing all training samples. We need to find the best feature and threshold to split this data. The goal is to create child nodes that are more 'pure' (contain mostly one class).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["Root Node
5 samples
3 Blue, 2 Red
Impurity: 0.48"]

steps.title: Evaluating Split Candidates
steps.explanation: For each feature, we try different **threshold values** and calculate the **information gain** (or Gini reduction). The split that best separates the classes wins.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["Root: 5 samples"] Root --> |"Try: X1 < 3.5"| Split1["Left: 3 samples
Right: 2 samples"] Root --> |"Try: X2 < 2.5"| Split2["Left: 2 samples
Right: 3 samples"] Split1 --> Best["Best Split!
Gini Gain: 0.32"] style Best fill:#90EE90

steps.title: First Split Applied
steps.explanation: We apply the best split `X1 < 3.5`. Samples with X1 < 3.5 go left, others go right. The left node is now **pure** (all Blue), but the right still needs splitting.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["X1 < 3.5?"] Root -->|Yes| Left["3 samples
3 Blue, 0 Red
PURE!"] Root -->|No| Right["2 samples
0 Blue, 2 Red
PURE!"] style Left fill:#ADD8E6 style Right fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Stopping Criteria
steps.explanation: A node becomes a **leaf** when: (1) it's pure (all same class), (2) max depth reached, (3) min samples threshold, or (4) no split improves purity. Leaf nodes make predictions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["X1 < 3.5?"] Root -->|Yes| Left["Predict: Blue
Confidence: 100%"] Root -->|No| Right["Predict: Red
Confidence: 100%"] style Left fill:#ADD8E6 style Right fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Making Predictions
steps.explanation: To predict a new sample, start at the root and follow the decision path. At each node, go left or right based on the split condition until you reach a leaf.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB New["New Sample
X=[2.5, 3]"] New --> Root["X1 < 3.5?"] Root -->|"2.5 < 3.5? Yes!"| Left["Predict: Blue"] Root -.->|No| Right["Predict: Red"] style New fill:#FFFACD style Left fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: Self-Attention Mechanism
fields.description: Step-by-step visualization of how self-attention works in transformers

steps.title: Input Embeddings
steps.explanation: Each token in the input sequence is converted to an **embedding vector**. These embeddings capture semantic meaning and are the starting point for attention computation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph Input Tokens
    T1[The]
    T2[cat]
    T3[sat]
    end
    subgraph Embeddings dim=4
    E1["[0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4]"]
    E2["[0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8]"]
    E3["[0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.5]"]
    end
    T1 --> E1
    T2 --> E2
    T3 --> E3

steps.title: Query, Key, Value Projections
steps.explanation: Each embedding is projected into three vectors: **Query (Q)**, **Key (K)**, and **Value (V)** using learned weight matrices. Q asks 'what am I looking for?', K says 'what do I contain?', V says 'what information do I provide?'
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    E["Embedding"]
    E --> |"W_Q"| Q["Query (Q)"]
    E --> |"W_K"| K["Key (K)"]
    E --> |"W_V"| V["Value (V)"]
    style Q fill:#FFB6C1
    style K fill:#ADD8E6
    style V fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Computing Attention Scores
steps.explanation: For each token, we compute how much it should 'attend' to every other token by taking the **dot product** of its Query with all Keys. Higher scores mean stronger attention.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Query from 'cat'"
    Q2["Q_cat"]
    end
    subgraph Keys
    K1["K_The"]
    K2["K_cat"]
    K3["K_sat"]
    end
    Q2 --> |"Q·K = 0.3"| K1
    Q2 --> |"Q·K = 0.8"| K2
    Q2 --> |"Q·K = 0.5"| K3

steps.title: Softmax Normalization
steps.explanation: The raw scores are scaled by √d_k (dimension of keys) and passed through **softmax** to get attention weights that sum to 1. This creates a probability distribution over tokens.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph Raw Scores
    S1["0.3"]
    S2["0.8"]
    S3["0.5"]
    end
    subgraph Softmax Weights
    W1["0.20"]
    W2["0.45"]
    W3["0.35"]
    end
    S1 --> |softmax| W1
    S2 --> |softmax| W2
    S3 --> |softmax| W3
    Note["Sum = 1.0"]

steps.title: Weighted Sum of Values
steps.explanation: The final output for each position is a **weighted sum** of all Value vectors, using the attention weights. Tokens with higher attention weights contribute more to the output.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph Values
    V1["V_The"]
    V2["V_cat"]
    V3["V_sat"]
    end
    subgraph Weights
    W1["0.20"]
    W2["0.45"]
    W3["0.35"]
    end
    Output["Output = 0.20·V_The + 0.45·V_cat + 0.35·V_sat"]
    V1 --> |"× 0.20"| Output
    V2 --> |"× 0.45"| Output
    V3 --> |"× 0.35"| Output
    style Output fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Multi-Head Attention
steps.explanation: In practice, we run **multiple attention heads** in parallel, each with its own Q, K, V projections. This allows the model to attend to different types of information simultaneously.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Input["Input Embeddings"] Input --> H1["Head 1
Syntax?"] Input --> H2["Head 2
Semantics?"] Input --> H3["Head 3
Position?"] H1 --> Concat["Concatenate"] H2 --> Concat H3 --> Concat Concat --> Output["Final Output"] style H1 fill:#FFB6C1 style H2 fill:#ADD8E6 style H3 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: LightGBM Leaf-wise Tree Growth
fields.description: See how LightGBM builds trees using leaf-wise (best-first) growth strategy

steps.title: Initial Prediction
steps.explanation: LightGBM starts with an **initial prediction** for all samples, typically the mean of target values. This becomes the baseline that subsequent trees will improve upon.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["Initial Prediction
F_0 = mean(y) = 0.5
100 samples"] style Root fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Calculate Residuals
steps.explanation: For each sample, calculate the **residual** (error): actual value minus current prediction. These residuals become the target for the next tree to learn.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph Current State
    P["Predictions: 0.5 for all"]
    end
    subgraph Residuals
    R1["Sample 1: 0.8 - 0.5 = 0.3"]
    R2["Sample 2: 0.2 - 0.5 = -0.3"]
    R3["Sample 3: 0.9 - 0.5 = 0.4"]
    Rn["..."]
    end
    P --> R1
    P --> R2
    P --> R3

steps.title: Find Best Split (Root)
steps.explanation: Unlike level-wise growth (XGBoost), LightGBM uses **leaf-wise** growth. It finds the leaf with the **highest potential gain** and splits that leaf, regardless of tree depth.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["Root: All Samples
Find best split across all features
Best: feature_3 < 0.7
Gain: 125.4"] Root --> |"< 0.7"| L1["Left Leaf
40 samples
Potential Gain: 45.2"] Root --> |">= 0.7"| R1["Right Leaf
60 samples
Potential Gain: 78.1"] style Root fill:#90EE90 style R1 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Split Leaf with Highest Gain
steps.explanation: LightGBM picks the leaf with highest potential gain (Right Leaf: 78.1) and splits it. This **greedy approach** often leads to deeper, more accurate trees than level-wise growth.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["feature_3 < 0.7"] Root --> L1["Left Leaf
40 samples"] Root --> R1["feature_1 < 2.3"] R1 --> |"Split!"| RL["30 samples
Gain: 52.3"] R1 --> RR["30 samples
Gain: 38.7"] style R1 fill:#90EE90 style RL fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Continue Leaf-wise Growth
steps.explanation: This process continues: evaluate all current leaves, pick the one with highest gain, and split it. The tree grows asymmetrically, focusing on areas with most error.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Root["feature_3 < 0.7"]
    Root --> L1["Leaf: 40 samples"]
    Root --> R1["feature_1 < 2.3"]
    R1 --> RL["feature_5 < 1.1"]
    R1 --> RR["Leaf: 30 samples"]
    RL --> RLL["Leaf: 15 samples"]
    RL --> RLR["Leaf: 15 samples"]
    style RL fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Stopping and Predictions
steps.explanation: Tree growth stops when: max leaves reached, max depth hit, or min gain threshold. Each leaf predicts a value to **reduce residuals**. The learning rate scales these predictions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Root["Tree Complete"]
    Root --> L1["Predict: +0.15"]
    Root --> R1["Split Node"]
    R1 --> RL["Split Node"]
    R1 --> RR["Predict: -0.22"]
    RL --> RLL["Predict: +0.31"]
    RL --> RLR["Predict: +0.08"]
    Update["F_1 = F_0 + lr × tree_predictions"]
    style Update fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Ensemble of Trees
steps.explanation: LightGBM builds many trees sequentially. Each new tree fits the residuals from all previous trees. The final prediction is the sum of initial value plus all tree contributions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR F0["F_0
Initial"] T1["Tree 1"] T2["Tree 2"] Tn["Tree N"] Final["Final Prediction
F_0 + lr×T1 + lr×T2 + ... + lr×TN"] F0 --> |+| T1 T1 --> |+| T2 T2 --> |+| Tn Tn --> Final style Final fill:#90EE90

fields.title: ML Model Serving Architecture
fields.description: Learn the components of a production ML model serving system

steps.title: Client Request
steps.explanation: A prediction request starts from a **client** (web app, mobile app, or internal service). The request contains input features needed for the model to make a prediction.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Client["Client App"] Client --> |"POST /predict
{features: [...]}"| API["API Gateway"] style Client fill:#ADD8E6 style API fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: API Gateway & Load Balancer
steps.explanation: The **API Gateway** handles authentication, rate limiting, and request validation. A **Load Balancer** distributes traffic across multiple model servers for high availability.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Client["Clients"] Client --> GW["API Gateway
Auth, Rate Limit"] GW --> LB["Load Balancer"] LB --> S1["Server 1"] LB --> S2["Server 2"] LB --> S3["Server 3"] style GW fill:#FFB6C1 style LB fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Feature Store Lookup
steps.explanation: The model server may need additional features from a **Feature Store**. This provides precomputed features (user history, aggregations) that aren't in the request.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Req["Request: user_id=123"] Req --> Server["Model Server"] Server --> |"Get features"| FS["Feature Store
(Redis/Feast)"] FS --> |"user_embedding,
purchase_history"| Server style FS fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Preprocessing Pipeline
steps.explanation: Raw features go through a **preprocessing pipeline**: normalization, encoding, missing value handling. This must match exactly what was used during training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Raw["Raw Features"] Raw --> Norm["Normalize
Numerical"] Norm --> Enc["Encode
Categorical"] Enc --> Fill["Fill
Missing"] Fill --> Vec["Feature
Vector"] style Vec fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Model Inference
steps.explanation: The preprocessed features are passed to the **ML model** for inference. The model is loaded in memory (or accessed via a serving framework like TensorFlow Serving, Triton, or custom).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Features["Feature Vector"] Features --> Model["ML Model
(In Memory)"] Model --> |"Inference"| Pred["Prediction
score: 0.87"] Cache["Model Cache
Version: v2.3"] Cache -.-> Model style Model fill:#ADD8E6 style Pred fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Postprocessing & Response
steps.explanation: The raw prediction may need **postprocessing**: applying thresholds, formatting output, adding explanations. The final response is sent back through the API.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Pred["Raw: 0.87"] Pred --> Post["Postprocess
threshold > 0.5"] Post --> Format["Format
Response"] Format --> Resp["{
prediction: 'positive',
confidence: 0.87
}"] style Resp fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Logging & Monitoring
steps.explanation: Every prediction is **logged** for debugging, monitoring, and model retraining. Metrics like latency, throughput, and prediction distribution are tracked.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Server["Model Server"] Server --> Log["Prediction Logs
(input, output, latency)"] Server --> Metrics["Metrics
(Prometheus)"] Log --> DW["Data Warehouse
(Retraining)"] Metrics --> Alert["Alerting
(PagerDuty)"] Metrics --> Dash["Dashboard
(Grafana)"] style Log fill:#FFFACD style Metrics fill:#E6E6FA

fields.title: Git Rebase: Rewriting History
fields.description: Learn how git rebase works and when to use it instead of merge

steps.title: Starting Point: Diverged Branches
steps.explanation: You have a **feature branch** that diverged from **main**. Both branches have new commits. This is a common scenario when working on a feature while others merge to main.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    branch feature
    checkout feature
    commit id: "C"
    commit id: "D"
    checkout main
    commit id: "E"
    commit id: "F" tag: "main"

steps.title: The Merge Approach
steps.explanation: With **git merge**, you create a merge commit that combines both histories. The commit history shows the parallel development. This preserves the true history but can create a cluttered log.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    branch feature
    checkout feature
    commit id: "C"
    commit id: "D"
    checkout main
    commit id: "E"
    commit id: "F"
    merge feature id: "M" tag: "merge commit"

steps.title: The Rebase Approach
steps.explanation: With **git rebase main**, you "replay" your feature commits on top of main. It's like saying: "pretend I started my work from the latest main." This creates a **linear history**.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    commit id: "E"
    commit id: "F" tag: "main"
    commit id: "C'" type: HIGHLIGHT
    commit id: "D'" tag: "feature" type: HIGHLIGHT

steps.title: How Rebase Works (Step 1)
steps.explanation: Git first finds the **common ancestor** (commit B) of your branch and the target branch. Then it temporarily saves your commits (C, D) as patches.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B" tag: "common ancestor"
    branch feature
    checkout feature
    commit id: "C (saved)"
    commit id: "D (saved)"
    checkout main
    commit id: "E"
    commit id: "F" tag: "rebase onto here"

steps.title: How Rebase Works (Step 2)
steps.explanation: Git moves your branch pointer to the tip of main (commit F). Then it **replays** each of your commits, one by one, creating new commits C' and D' with new hashes.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    commit id: "E"
    commit id: "F"
    commit id: "C' (replayed)" type: HIGHLIGHT
    commit id: "D' (replayed)" tag: "feature" type: HIGHLIGHT

steps.title: Handling Conflicts
steps.explanation: If a conflict occurs during replay, Git pauses and lets you resolve it. After fixing conflicts: **git add .** then **git rebase --continue**. Use **git rebase --abort** to cancel.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD
    A[git rebase main] --> B{Conflict?}
    B -->|No| C[Commit replayed]
    B -->|Yes| D[CONFLICT - Rebase paused]
    D --> E[Fix conflicts manually]
    E --> F[git add .]
    F --> G[git rebase --continue]
    G --> B
    D --> H[git rebase --abort]
    H --> I[Back to original state]
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
    style G fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Interactive Rebase: Squashing Commits
steps.explanation: Use **git rebase -i HEAD~3** to modify the last 3 commits. You can **squash** (combine), **reword** (edit message), **edit** (amend), **drop** (delete), or **reorder** commits.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Before
    A1["fix typo"]
    A2["add feature"]
    A3["fix typo again"]
    end
    subgraph "git rebase -i"
    B1["pick: add feature"]
    B2["squash: fix typo"]
    B3["squash: fix typo again"]
    end
    subgraph After
    C1["add feature (clean)"]
    end
    Before --> B1
    B1 --> After
    style C1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The Golden Rule of Rebasing
steps.explanation: **Never rebase commits that have been pushed and shared with others.** Rebase rewrites history (new commit hashes). If others based work on the old commits, you'll cause conflicts and confusion.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Safe to Rebase"
    A[Local commits not pushed]
    B[Your own feature branch]
    C[Before opening PR]
    end
    subgraph "NEVER Rebase"
    D[Commits on main/master]
    E[Shared branches]
    F[After PR is merged]
    end
    style A fill:#90EE90
    style B fill:#90EE90
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
    style E fill:#FFB6C1
    style F fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Rebase vs Merge: When to Use Each
steps.explanation: Use **rebase** for: cleaning up local commits, keeping feature branch up-to-date, linear history preference. Use **merge** for: preserving complete history, shared branches, when history matters.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD Q{What are you doing?} Q -->|"Updating feature branch
with latest main"| R1["git rebase main
(if not shared)"] Q -->|"Integrating feature
into main"| R2["git merge feature
(preserves history)"] Q -->|"Cleaning up commits
before PR"| R3["git rebase -i
(squash/reword)"] Q -->|"Shared branch or
already pushed"| R4["git merge
(safer)"] style R1 fill:#ADD8E6 style R2 fill:#90EE90 style R3 fill:#ADD8E6 style R4 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Git Reset: Soft, Mixed, and Hard
fields.description: Understand the three reset modes and how they affect HEAD, staging, and working directory

steps.title: The Three Trees of Git
steps.explanation: Git manages three "trees" (file collections): **Working Directory** (your files), **Staging Area/Index** (next commit), and **Repository/HEAD** (last commit). Understanding these is key to understanding reset.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Three Trees" WD["Working Directory
(Your Files)"] SA["Staging Area
(Index)"] REPO["Repository
(HEAD Commit)"] end WD -->|git add| SA SA -->|git commit| REPO style WD fill:#90EE90 style SA fill:#FFFACD style REPO fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Starting State
steps.explanation: Let's say you have 3 commits (A→B→C) and HEAD points to C. All three trees are in sync - working directory, staging area, and HEAD all have the same content.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    commit id: "C" tag: "HEAD"

steps.title: Git Reset --soft
steps.explanation: **--soft** only moves HEAD to the target commit. Staging area and working directory are **untouched**. Your changes appear as "staged for commit". Use this to redo a commit message or combine commits.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "After: git reset --soft B" HEAD2["HEAD → B"] STAGE2["Staging: C's changes
(ready to commit)"] WORK2["Working Dir: C's changes"] end subgraph Legend L1["Moved"] L2["Unchanged"] end style HEAD2 fill:#FFB6C1 style STAGE2 fill:#90EE90 style WORK2 fill:#90EE90 style L1 fill:#FFB6C1 style L2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Soft Reset Use Case
steps.explanation: Made a commit but want to change the message or add more files? Use **git reset --soft HEAD~1** to undo the commit but keep everything staged. Then commit again with the right message.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    A["Oops, wrong commit message"] --> B["git reset --soft HEAD~1"]
    B --> C["Changes still staged"]
    C --> D["git commit -m 'Better message'"]
    style D fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Git Reset --mixed (Default)
steps.explanation: **--mixed** (the default) moves HEAD and resets the staging area, but leaves working directory **untouched**. Changes appear as "unstaged". Use this to unstage files or reorganize what goes into commits.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "After: git reset --mixed B" HEAD3["HEAD → B"] STAGE3["Staging: Empty
(matches B)"] WORK3["Working Dir: C's changes
(unstaged)"] end subgraph Legend L1["Moved/Reset"] L2["Unchanged"] end style HEAD3 fill:#FFB6C1 style STAGE3 fill:#FFB6C1 style WORK3 fill:#90EE90 style L1 fill:#FFB6C1 style L2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Mixed Reset Use Case
steps.explanation: Accidentally staged files you didn't want? Or want to split a big commit into smaller ones? Use **git reset HEAD~1** (mixed is default) to unstage and re-add files selectively.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    A["Committed too many files"] --> B["git reset HEAD~1"]
    B --> C["All changes unstaged"]
    C --> D["git add file1.py"]
    D --> E["git commit -m 'First part'"]
    E --> F["git add file2.py"]
    F --> G["git commit -m 'Second part'"]
    style E fill:#90EE90
    style G fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Git Reset --hard
steps.explanation: **--hard** moves HEAD, resets staging area, AND resets working directory. All changes are **discarded**. This is destructive - uncommitted work is lost! Use with caution.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "After: git reset --hard B" HEAD4["HEAD → B"] STAGE4["Staging: Empty
(matches B)"] WORK4["Working Dir: B's content
(C's changes GONE)"] end subgraph Legend L1["Moved/Reset"] L2["DESTROYED"] end style HEAD4 fill:#FFB6C1 style STAGE4 fill:#FFB6C1 style WORK4 fill:#FFB6C1 style L1 fill:#FFB6C1 style L2 fill:#FF0000

steps.title: Hard Reset Use Case
steps.explanation: Want to completely abandon recent work and go back? **git reset --hard HEAD~2** throws away the last 2 commits entirely. Or **git reset --hard origin/main** to match the remote exactly.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    A["Made a mess of things"] --> B["git reset --hard origin/main"]
    B --> C["Local matches remote exactly"]
    C --> D["All local changes gone"]
    style D fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Comparison Summary
steps.explanation: Quick reference: **--soft** keeps everything, **--mixed** unstages but keeps files, **--hard** destroys everything. When in doubt, start with --soft - you can always go harder.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "git reset --soft"
    S1["HEAD: Moved"]
    S2["Staging: Unchanged"]
    S3["Working: Unchanged"]
    end
    subgraph "git reset --mixed"
    M1["HEAD: Moved"]
    M2["Staging: Reset"]
    M3["Working: Unchanged"]
    end
    subgraph "git reset --hard"
    H1["HEAD: Moved"]
    H2["Staging: Reset"]
    H3["Working: Reset"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style S2 fill:#90EE90
    style S3 fill:#90EE90
    style M1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style M2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style M3 fill:#90EE90
    style H1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style H2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style H3 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Recovery with Reflog
steps.explanation: Accidentally did a hard reset? Git reflog tracks all HEAD movements. Find the lost commit hash with **git reflog** and recover with **git reset --hard <hash>**. Commits aren't truly gone until garbage collected (~30 days).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD
    A["Oops! git reset --hard"] --> B["git reflog"]
    B --> C["See: abc123 HEAD@{1}: commit: My work"]
    C --> D["git reset --hard abc123"]
    D --> E["Work recovered!"]
    style A fill:#FFB6C1
    style E fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Git Fetch vs Pull
fields.description: Understand the difference between fetch and pull, and when to use each

steps.title: The Setup: Local and Remote
steps.explanation: You have a **local repository** on your machine and a **remote repository** (like GitHub). Your local repo has **remote-tracking branches** (like origin/main) that remember where the remote branches were last time you checked.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Your Machine" LOCAL["Local Repo
main branch"] TRACK["origin/main
(tracking branch)"] end subgraph "GitHub" REMOTE["Remote Repo
main branch"] end TRACK -.->|"tracks"| REMOTE style LOCAL fill:#90EE90 style TRACK fill:#FFFACD style REMOTE fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Starting State
steps.explanation: Both local and remote are at commit B. Someone else pushes commit C to the remote. Now the remote is ahead, but your local repo doesn't know yet.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Local (outdated)"
    L1["A"] --> L2["B"]
    L2 --> LH["main, origin/main"]
    end
    subgraph "Remote (ahead)"
    R1["A"] --> R2["B"] --> R3["C"]
    R3 --> RH["main"]
    end
    style R3 fill:#90EE90
    style LH fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Git Fetch: Download Only
steps.explanation: **git fetch** downloads new commits from the remote and updates your **remote-tracking branch** (origin/main). It does NOT touch your local main branch. Safe and non-destructive.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "After: git fetch"
    L1["A"] --> L2["B"]
    L2 --> LH["main (unchanged)"]
    L2 --> L3["C"]
    L3 --> OH["origin/main (updated)"]
    end
    style L3 fill:#90EE90
    style OH fill:#90EE90
    style LH fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: After Fetch: You Decide
steps.explanation: After fetching, you can inspect what changed before merging. Use **git log origin/main** or **git diff main origin/main** to see what's new. Then merge when ready.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD
    A["git fetch origin"] --> B["origin/main updated"]
    B --> C{"Review changes?"}
    C -->|"git log origin/main"| D["See new commits"]
    C -->|"git diff main origin/main"| E["See code changes"]
    D --> F["git merge origin/main"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Local main updated"]
    style A fill:#ADD8E6
    style G fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Git Pull: Fetch + Merge
steps.explanation: **git pull** is simply **git fetch** followed by **git merge**. It downloads AND immediately integrates the changes into your current branch. Convenient but gives you less control.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "git pull = fetch + merge"
    FETCH["git fetch"]
    MERGE["git merge origin/main"]
    FETCH --> MERGE
    end
    style FETCH fill:#ADD8E6
    style MERGE fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Pull Result
steps.explanation: After **git pull**, your local main is updated to include the remote changes. If there were no local commits, it's a simple fast-forward. Otherwise, a merge commit may be created.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "After: git pull (fast-forward)"
    L1["A"] --> L2["B"] --> L3["C"]
    L3 --> LH["main, origin/main"]
    end
    style L3 fill:#90EE90
    style LH fill:#90EE90

steps.title: When Branches Diverge
steps.explanation: If you have local commits AND the remote has new commits, **git pull** will create a **merge commit**. This can clutter history. Some teams prefer **git pull --rebase** for a linear history.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Before Pull (diverged)"
    B1["A"] --> B2["B"]
    B2 --> B3["C (local)"]
    B2 --> B4["D (remote)"]
    end
    subgraph "After git pull"
    A1["A"] --> A2["B"]
    A2 --> A3["C"]
    A2 --> A4["D"]
    A3 --> A5["M (merge)"]
    A4 --> A5
    end
    style A5 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Git Pull --rebase
steps.explanation: **git pull --rebase** fetches then rebases your local commits on top of the remote changes. This creates a linear history without merge commits. Popular for keeping a clean git log.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Before"
    B1["A"] --> B2["B"]
    B2 --> B3["C (local)"]
    B2 --> B4["D (remote)"]
    end
    subgraph "After git pull --rebase"
    A1["A"] --> A2["B"] --> A4["D"] --> A3["C' (rebased)"]
    end
    style A3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: When to Use Each
steps.explanation: Use **fetch** when: you want to review changes first, working on shared branch, or CI/CD scripts. Use **pull** when: you trust incoming changes, working alone, want convenience. Use **pull --rebase** for linear history.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD Q{"What do you need?"} Q -->|"Review before integrating"| F["git fetch
then git merge"] Q -->|"Quick update, trust remote"| P["git pull"] Q -->|"Update + linear history"| PR["git pull --rebase"] Q -->|"Just see what's new"| FO["git fetch
git log origin/main"] style F fill:#ADD8E6 style P fill:#90EE90 style PR fill:#FFFACD style FO fill:#E6E6FA

fields.title: Git Cherry-pick: Selective Commit Copying
fields.description: Learn how to copy specific commits from one branch to another with cherry-pick

steps.title: What is Cherry-pick?
steps.explanation: **Git cherry-pick** copies a specific commit from one branch and applies it to another branch. Unlike merge (which brings all commits) or rebase (which moves your branch), cherry-pick lets you pick individual commits.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Cherry-pick Concept"
    TREE["Branch with many commits"]
    CHERRY["Pick ONE specific commit"]
    TARGET["Apply to your branch"]
    end
    TREE --> CHERRY --> TARGET
    style CHERRY fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Starting Scenario
steps.explanation: You're on **main** branch and need a specific bug fix (commit D) from the **feature** branch, but you don't want all the feature commits yet. This is a perfect use case for cherry-pick.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B" tag: "main"
    branch feature
    checkout feature
    commit id: "C" type: NORMAL
    commit id: "D (bug fix)" type: HIGHLIGHT
    commit id: "E" type: NORMAL

steps.title: Find the Commit Hash
steps.explanation: First, identify the commit you want to cherry-pick using **git log**. Each commit has a unique SHA hash. You can use the full hash or the first 7 characters.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD
    A["git log feature --oneline"] --> B["Output:"]
    B --> C["abc123e E - Add feature tests"]
    B --> D["def456d D - Fix critical bug"]
    B --> E["ghi789c C - Start feature work"]
    D --> F["Copy: def456d"]
    style D fill:#90EE90
    style F fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Execute Cherry-pick
steps.explanation: Run **git cherry-pick <commit-hash>** while on your target branch. Git creates a NEW commit with the same changes but a different hash. The original commit stays on its branch.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    branch feature
    checkout feature
    commit id: "C"
    commit id: "D (bug fix)"
    commit id: "E"
    checkout main
    commit id: "D'" tag: "main" type: HIGHLIGHT

steps.title: Cherry-pick vs Original
steps.explanation: The cherry-picked commit (D') has the **same code changes** and **same commit message** as the original (D), but a **different SHA hash** because it has different parent commits. They're copies, not the same commit.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Feature Branch" D["Commit D
SHA: def456d
Parent: C"] end subgraph "Main Branch" DP["Commit D'
SHA: xyz789a
Parent: B"] end D -->|"Same changes
Different hash"| DP style D fill:#ADD8E6 style DP fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Cherry-picking Multiple Commits
steps.explanation: You can cherry-pick multiple commits in sequence. List them individually or use a range. Order matters - commits are applied in the order you specify.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Single commits"
    S1["git cherry-pick abc123"]
    S2["git cherry-pick def456 ghi789"]
    end
    subgraph "Range of commits"
    R1["git cherry-pick abc123..def456"]
    R2["(excludes abc123, includes def456)"]
    end
    subgraph "Range inclusive"
    I1["git cherry-pick abc123^..def456"]
    I2["(includes both endpoints)"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#ADD8E6
    style R1 fill:#FFFACD
    style I1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Handling Conflicts
steps.explanation: If the cherry-picked changes conflict with your branch, Git pauses and asks you to resolve them. After fixing conflicts, use **git add** then **git cherry-pick --continue**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD
    A["git cherry-pick abc123"] --> B{Conflict?}
    B -->|No| C["Commit created automatically"]
    B -->|Yes| D["CONFLICT - Cherry-pick paused"]
    D --> E["Fix conflicts in files"]
    E --> F["git add ."]
    F --> G["git cherry-pick --continue"]
    G --> C
    D --> H["git cherry-pick --abort"]
    H --> I["Cancel and restore original state"]
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style H fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Useful Options
steps.explanation: Cherry-pick has helpful options: **-n / --no-commit** stages changes without committing (useful for combining), **-x** adds source commit info to message, **-e** lets you edit the commit message.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Common Options" O1["--no-commit (-n)
Stage changes only, don't commit"] O2["-x
Add 'cherry picked from...' to message"] O3["--edit (-e)
Edit commit message before committing"] O4["--signoff (-s)
Add Signed-off-by line"] end subgraph "Example" E1["git cherry-pick -x abc123"] E2["Message includes:
cherry picked from commit abc123"] end O2 --> E1 --> E2 style O1 fill:#ADD8E6 style O2 fill:#90EE90 style O3 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Common Use Cases
steps.explanation: Cherry-pick is ideal for: **hotfixes** (apply bug fix to release branch), **backporting** (bring feature to older version), **selective integration** (pick specific commits from PRs), and **undoing mistakes** (revert then cherry-pick good commits).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Hotfix Scenario"
    H1["Bug found in production"]
    H2["Fix made on develop"]
    H3["Cherry-pick fix to release branch"]
    H1 --> H2 --> H3
    end
    subgraph "Backport Scenario"
    B1["New feature on main"]
    B2["Customer needs it on v2.x"]
    B3["Cherry-pick to release/2.x"]
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end
    style H3 fill:#90EE90
    style B3 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Cherry-pick vs Merge vs Rebase
steps.explanation: **Merge** brings all commits and preserves branch history. **Rebase** moves entire branch to new base. **Cherry-pick** copies specific commits individually. Use cherry-pick when you need surgical precision.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD Q{"What do you need?"} Q -->|"All commits from branch"| M["git merge
Preserves history"] Q -->|"Move branch to new base"| R["git rebase
Linear history"] Q -->|"Just specific commits"| C["git cherry-pick
Selective copying"] subgraph "Cherry-pick is for" C1["Hotfixes"] C2["Backports"] C3["Selective features"] end C --> C1 C --> C2 C --> C3 style M fill:#ADD8E6 style R fill:#FFFACD style C fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Potential Pitfalls
steps.explanation: Be careful: cherry-picking creates **duplicate commits** (same changes, different hashes). If you later merge the original branch, you may see the changes twice or get conflicts. Document cherry-picks with **-x** flag.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Warning: Duplicate Commits"
    W1["Cherry-pick D to main as D'"]
    W2["Later merge feature to main"]
    W3["Git sees D and D' as different"]
    W4["May cause conflicts or duplicates"]
    end
    W1 --> W2 --> W3 --> W4
    subgraph "Best Practices"
    BP1["Use -x flag for traceability"]
    BP2["Document in PR/commit message"]
    BP3["Consider if merge is better"]
    end
    style W4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style BP1 fill:#90EE90
    style BP2 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Isolation Forest: Anomaly Detection
fields.description: Learn how Isolation Forest detects anomalies by isolating observations using random splits

steps.title: The Problem: Anomaly Detection
steps.explanation: **Anomaly detection** finds data points that are significantly different from the majority. Use cases include fraud detection, network intrusion, manufacturing defects, and medical diagnosis. Traditional methods struggle with high-dimensional data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Normal Data (Majority)"
    N1["Transaction: $50"]
    N2["Transaction: $75"]
    N3["Transaction: $60"]
    N4["Transaction: $45"]
    end
    subgraph "Anomalies (Rare)"
    A1["Transaction: $50,000"]
    A2["Transaction: $0.01"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style A2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style N1 fill:#90EE90
    style N2 fill:#90EE90
    style N3 fill:#90EE90
    style N4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Key Insight: Anomalies are Easy to Isolate
steps.explanation: Isolation Forest is based on a simple observation: **anomalies are few and different**. Because they're rare and have unusual feature values, they can be isolated with fewer random splits than normal points.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "2D Feature Space" direction TB Cluster["Normal points
(clustered together)"] Outlier["Anomaly
(isolated)"] end Cluster --> |"Hard to isolate
Many splits needed"| C1["Deep in tree"] Outlier --> |"Easy to isolate
Few splits needed"| O1["Near root"] style Outlier fill:#FFB6C1 style Cluster fill:#90EE90 style O1 fill:#FFB6C1 style C1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Building an Isolation Tree
steps.explanation: An **Isolation Tree (iTree)** is built by randomly selecting a feature and a random split value between the feature's min and max. This process repeats recursively until each point is isolated or max depth is reached.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["All Data (100 points)"] Root --> |"Random: Feature X < 0.4"| L1["Left: 60 points"] Root --> |"Feature X >= 0.4"| R1["Right: 40 points"] L1 --> |"Random: Feature Y < 0.7"| L2["35 points"] L1 --> |"Feature Y >= 0.7"| R2["25 points"] R1 --> |"Random: Feature Z < 0.2"| L3["15 points"] R1 --> |"Feature Z >= 0.2"| R3["25 points"] style Root fill:#E6E6FA Note["Splits are RANDOM
Not optimized like Decision Trees"]

steps.title: Isolating a Normal Point
steps.explanation: A **normal point** lives in a dense region with many similar points. It takes **many random splits** to finally isolate it from its neighbors. This results in a **long path** from root to leaf.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["Start"] --> S1["Split 1"] S1 --> S2["Split 2"] S2 --> S3["Split 3"] S3 --> S4["Split 4"] S4 --> S5["Split 5"] S5 --> S6["Split 6"] S6 --> Leaf["Normal Point
Path Length = 6"] style Leaf fill:#90EE90 style Root fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Isolating an Anomaly
steps.explanation: An **anomaly** has unusual feature values that make it easy to separate from the rest. A random split is likely to isolate it quickly, resulting in a **short path** from root to leaf.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["Start"] --> S1["Split 1"] S1 --> Leaf["Anomaly!
Path Length = 1"] S1 --> Continue["...rest of tree..."] style Leaf fill:#FFB6C1 style Root fill:#E6E6FA style Continue fill:#EEEEEE

steps.title: Path Length Comparison
steps.explanation: The **path length** (number of edges from root to leaf) is the key metric. Anomalies have **shorter average path lengths** across multiple trees. This is the basis for the anomaly score.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Normal Point" NP["Path lengths:
6, 7, 5, 8, 6
Average: 6.4"] end subgraph "Anomaly" AP["Path lengths:
2, 1, 3, 2, 1
Average: 1.8"] end NP --> N["Low anomaly score"] AP --> A["HIGH anomaly score"] style AP fill:#FFB6C1 style A fill:#FFB6C1 style NP fill:#90EE90 style N fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Building the Forest
steps.explanation: Isolation Forest builds **multiple trees** (default: 100), each trained on a random subsample of data (default: 256 points). This ensemble approach reduces variance and improves robustness.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Data["Full Dataset
10,000 points"] Data --> |"Sample 256"| T1["iTree 1"] Data --> |"Sample 256"| T2["iTree 2"] Data --> |"Sample 256"| T3["iTree 3"] Data --> |"..."| Tn["iTree 100"] T1 --> Ensemble["Isolation Forest"] T2 --> Ensemble T3 --> Ensemble Tn --> Ensemble style Ensemble fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Computing the Anomaly Score
steps.explanation: The anomaly score is computed from the **average path length** across all trees, normalized by the expected path length for the sample size. Scores range from 0 to 1, where **scores close to 1 indicate anomalies**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "For each point" PL["Get path length
from each tree"] AVG["Calculate average
path length E(h)"] NORM["Normalize by
expected length c(n)"] SCORE["Score = 2^(-E(h)/c(n))"] end PL --> AVG --> NORM --> SCORE subgraph "Score Interpretation" S1["Score → 1: Anomaly"] S2["Score → 0.5: Normal"] S3["Score → 0: Very normal"] end style S1 fill:#FFB6C1 style S2 fill:#FFFACD style S3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Setting the Contamination Parameter
steps.explanation: The **contamination** parameter (default: 'auto') sets the expected proportion of anomalies. It determines the threshold for classifying points as anomalies. Set it based on domain knowledge or cross-validation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "contamination=0.1 (10% anomalies)" S1["Scores > threshold → Anomaly"] S2["Top 10% of scores
flagged as anomalies"] end subgraph "Effect on Threshold" C1["contamination=0.01"] --> T1["High threshold
Few anomalies"] C2["contamination=0.1"] --> T2["Medium threshold
More anomalies"] C3["contamination=0.5"] --> T3["Low threshold
Many anomalies"] end style T1 fill:#90EE90 style T2 fill:#FFFACD style T3 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: scikit-learn Implementation
steps.explanation: Using Isolation Forest in scikit-learn is straightforward. Key parameters: **n_estimators** (trees), **max_samples** (subsample size), **contamination** (anomaly proportion), **random_state** (reproducibility).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Code Flow" C1["from sklearn.ensemble
import IsolationForest"] C2["clf = IsolationForest(
n_estimators=100,
contamination=0.1)"] C3["clf.fit(X_train)"] C4["predictions = clf.predict(X)
-1 = anomaly, 1 = normal"] C5["scores = clf.score_samples(X)
lower = more anomalous"] end C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 C3 --> C5 style C4 fill:#90EE90 style C5 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Strengths and Limitations
steps.explanation: **Strengths**: Fast training O(n), handles high dimensions well, no distance calculations needed, works without labels. **Limitations**: Struggles with local anomalies in dense regions, assumes anomalies are globally isolated.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Strengths"
    S1["Fast & Scalable"]
    S2["High-dimensional data"]
    S3["No labels needed"]
    S4["Memory efficient"]
    end
    subgraph "Limitations"
    L1["Global isolation assumption"]
    L2["Axis-parallel splits only"]
    L3["Sensitive to contamination"]
    end
    subgraph "Best Use Cases"
    U1["Fraud detection"]
    U2["Network intrusion"]
    U3["Sensor anomalies"]
    U4["Data cleaning"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#90EE90
    style S2 fill:#90EE90
    style S3 fill:#90EE90
    style S4 fill:#90EE90
    style L1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style L2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style L3 fill:#FFB6C1
    style U1 fill:#ADD8E6
    style U2 fill:#ADD8E6
    style U3 fill:#ADD8E6
    style U4 fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: LightGBM Tree Growth: Step by Step
fields.description: Watch a LightGBM tree grow leaf-by-leaf over multiple iterations

steps.title: Setup: The Dataset
steps.explanation: We have a regression dataset with 1000 samples and 10 features. LightGBM will build a tree to predict the residuals (errors) from the current model. Let's watch one tree grow leaf-by-leaf.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Dataset"
    D1["1000 samples"]
    D2["10 features (X1-X10)"]
    D3["Target: residuals"]
    end
    subgraph "LightGBM Settings"
    S1["num_leaves = 8"]
    S2["max_depth = -1 (unlimited)"]
    S3["min_data_in_leaf = 20"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#ADD8E6
    style S1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 1: Root Node
steps.explanation: The tree starts with a single **root node** containing all 1000 samples. LightGBM calculates the best split across all features using histogram-based splitting. The node predicts the mean residual.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["ROOT
1000 samples
Prediction: 0.0
Gain potential: 156.3"] style Root fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Step 2: First Split
steps.explanation: LightGBM finds the best split: **X3 < 0.45**. This creates two leaf nodes. Each leaf calculates its optimal prediction value to minimize the loss function (e.g., MSE for regression).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["X3 < 0.45?"] Root -->|Yes| L1["Leaf 1
420 samples
Pred: -0.23
Gain: 45.2"] Root -->|No| L2["Leaf 2
580 samples
Pred: +0.17
Gain: 62.8"] style Root fill:#E6E6FA style L1 fill:#90EE90 style L2 fill:#90EE90 Note["2 leaves total"]

steps.title: Step 3: Choose Best Leaf to Split
steps.explanation: **Key difference from XGBoost**: LightGBM uses **leaf-wise** growth. It evaluates ALL current leaves and splits the one with the **highest gain**. Leaf 2 (gain=62.8) > Leaf 1 (gain=45.2), so Leaf 2 gets split.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["X3 < 0.45?"] Root -->|Yes| L1["Leaf 1
Gain: 45.2"] Root -->|No| L2["Leaf 2
Gain: 62.8
SPLIT THIS!"] style L1 fill:#FFFACD style L2 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Step 4: Second Split
steps.explanation: Leaf 2 is split on **X7 < 0.82**. Now we have 3 leaves. Notice the tree is growing **asymmetrically** - we're going deeper on the right side because that's where the gain is highest.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["X3 < 0.45?"] Root -->|Yes| L1["Leaf 1
420 samples
Gain: 45.2"] Root -->|No| N2["X7 < 0.82?"] N2 -->|Yes| L3["Leaf 3
310 samples
Pred: +0.08
Gain: 38.5"] N2 -->|No| L4["Leaf 4
270 samples
Pred: +0.28
Gain: 51.3"] style N2 fill:#E6E6FA style L3 fill:#90EE90 style L4 fill:#90EE90 Note["3 leaves total"]

steps.title: Step 5: Third Split
steps.explanation: Compare gains: Leaf 4 (51.3) > Leaf 1 (45.2) > Leaf 3 (38.5). Leaf 4 wins! It gets split on **X1 < 0.33**. The tree continues to grow where errors are largest.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["X3 < 0.45?"] Root -->|Yes| L1["Leaf 1
420 samples
Gain: 45.2"] Root -->|No| N2["X7 < 0.82?"] N2 -->|Yes| L3["Leaf 3
310 samples
Gain: 38.5"] N2 -->|No| N4["X1 < 0.33?"] N4 -->|Yes| L5["Leaf 5
145 samples
Pred: +0.41
Gain: 28.7"] N4 -->|No| L6["Leaf 6
125 samples
Pred: +0.19
Gain: 33.1"] style N4 fill:#E6E6FA style L5 fill:#90EE90 style L6 fill:#90EE90 Note["4 leaves total"]

steps.title: Step 6: Fourth Split
steps.explanation: Gains: Leaf 1 (45.2) > Leaf 3 (38.5) > Leaf 6 (33.1) > Leaf 5 (28.7). Finally Leaf 1 (the original left branch) gets split on **X5 < 0.67**. The tree is becoming more balanced.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["X3 < 0.45?"] Root -->|Yes| N1["X5 < 0.67?"] N1 -->|Yes| L7["Leaf 7
185 samples
Pred: -0.35"] N1 -->|No| L8["Leaf 8
235 samples
Pred: -0.14"] Root -->|No| N2["X7 < 0.82?"] N2 -->|Yes| L3["Leaf 3
310 samples"] N2 -->|No| N4["X1 < 0.33?"] N4 -->|Yes| L5["Leaf 5
145 samples"] N4 -->|No| L6["Leaf 6
125 samples"] style N1 fill:#E6E6FA style L7 fill:#90EE90 style L8 fill:#90EE90 Note["5 leaves total"]

steps.title: Step 7: Fifth Split
steps.explanation: Leaf 3 has the highest remaining gain. Split on **X9 < 0.51**. We now have 6 leaves. Each split reduces the overall prediction error on training data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["X3 < 0.45?"] Root -->|Yes| N1["X5 < 0.67?"] N1 -->|Yes| L7["Leaf 7"] N1 -->|No| L8["Leaf 8"] Root -->|No| N2["X7 < 0.82?"] N2 -->|Yes| N3["X9 < 0.51?"] N3 -->|Yes| L9["Leaf 9
165 samples"] N3 -->|No| L10["Leaf 10
145 samples"] N2 -->|No| N4["X1 < 0.33?"] N4 -->|Yes| L5["Leaf 5"] N4 -->|No| L6["Leaf 6"] style N3 fill:#E6E6FA style L9 fill:#90EE90 style L10 fill:#90EE90 Note["6 leaves total"]

steps.title: Step 8: Sixth Split
steps.explanation: Continue splitting the leaf with highest gain. Leaf 8 is split on **X2 < 0.28**. We now have 7 leaves - one more to go to reach our num_leaves=8 limit.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["X3 < 0.45?"] Root -->|Yes| N1["X5 < 0.67?"] N1 -->|Yes| L7["Leaf 7"] N1 -->|No| N8["X2 < 0.28?"] N8 -->|Yes| L11["Leaf 11
98 samples"] N8 -->|No| L12["Leaf 12
137 samples"] Root -->|No| N2["X7 < 0.82?"] N2 -->|Yes| N3["X9 < 0.51?"] N3 -->|Yes| L9["Leaf 9"] N3 -->|No| L10["Leaf 10"] N2 -->|No| N4["X1 < 0.33?"] N4 -->|Yes| L5["Leaf 5"] N4 -->|No| L6["Leaf 6"] style N8 fill:#E6E6FA style L11 fill:#90EE90 style L12 fill:#90EE90 Note["7 leaves total"]

steps.title: Step 9: Final Split (num_leaves = 8)
steps.explanation: Last split! Leaf 5 is split on **X4 < 0.71**. We've reached **num_leaves=8**, so tree growth stops. Each leaf now contains a prediction value that will be scaled by the learning rate.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Root["X3 < 0.45?"] Root -->|Yes| N1["X5 < 0.67?"] N1 -->|Yes| L7["Leaf 7
pred: -0.35"] N1 -->|No| N8["X2 < 0.28?"] N8 -->|Yes| L11["Leaf 11
pred: -0.21"] N8 -->|No| L12["Leaf 12
pred: -0.08"] Root -->|No| N2["X7 < 0.82?"] N2 -->|Yes| N3["X9 < 0.51?"] N3 -->|Yes| L9["Leaf 9

pred: +0.12"] N2 -->|No| N4["X1 < 0.33?"] N4 -->|Yes| N5["X4 < 0.71?"] N5 -->|Yes| L13["Leaf 13
pred: +0.52"] N5 -->|No| L14["Leaf 14
pred: +0.31"] N4 -->|No| L6["Leaf 6
pred: +0.19"] style L7 fill:#ADD8E6 style L11 fill:#ADD8E6 style L12 fill:#ADD8E6 style L9 fill:#90EE90 style L10 fill:#90EE90 style L13 fill:#90EE90 style L14 fill:#90EE90 style L6 fill:#90EE90 Note["8 leaves - COMPLETE!"]

steps.title: Tree Complete: Make Predictions
steps.explanation: To predict for a new sample, traverse from root to leaf following the split conditions. The leaf's prediction is multiplied by the **learning_rate** (e.g., 0.1) and added to the current model's prediction.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "New Sample: X3=0.6, X7=0.9, X1=0.4, X4=0.5" Q1["X3 < 0.45?

0.4 < 0.33? NO"] Q3 -->|No| LEAF["Leaf 6
pred: +0.19"] end LEAF --> FINAL["Final contribution:
0.1 × 0.19 = +0.019"] style LEAF fill:#90EE90 style FINAL fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Leaf-wise vs Level-wise Growth
steps.explanation: **Level-wise** (XGBoost default) grows all nodes at same depth before going deeper - creates balanced trees. **Leaf-wise** (LightGBM) always splits the best leaf - creates asymmetric but often more accurate trees.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Level-wise (XGBoost)"
    direction TB
    XR["Root"]
    XR --> XL1["L1"]
    XR --> XL2["L2"]
    XL1 --> XL3["L3"]
    XL1 --> XL4["L4"]
    XL2 --> XL5["L5"]
    XL2 --> XL6["L6"]
    end
    subgraph "Leaf-wise (LightGBM)"
    direction TB
    LR["Root"]
    LR --> LL1["L1"]
    LR --> LN2["Node"]
    LN2 --> LN3["Node"]
    LN2 --> LL4["L4"]
    LN3 --> LL5["L5"]
    LN3 --> LL6["L6"]
    end
    style XL3 fill:#ADD8E6
    style XL4 fill:#ADD8E6
    style XL5 fill:#ADD8E6
    style XL6 fill:#ADD8E6
    style LL1 fill:#90EE90
    style LL4 fill:#90EE90
    style LL5 fill:#90EE90
    style LL6 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Key Parameters for Tree Growth
steps.explanation: Control tree growth with these key parameters: **num_leaves** (max leaves per tree), **max_depth** (limit depth to prevent overfitting), **min_data_in_leaf** (minimum samples per leaf), **min_gain_to_split** (minimum gain required).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Stopping Conditions" S1["num_leaves reached
(e.g., 31)"] S2["max_depth reached
(e.g., 6)"] S3["min_data_in_leaf
(e.g., 20 samples)"] S4["min_gain_to_split
(e.g., 0.1)"] end subgraph "Overfitting Risk" O1["More leaves = More complex"] O2["Deeper = More specific"] O3["Fewer min samples = Overfit"] end S1 --> O1 S2 --> O2 S3 --> O3 style S1 fill:#90EE90 style S2 fill:#ADD8E6 style S3 fill:#FFFACD style O1 fill:#FFB6C1 style O2 fill:#FFB6C1 style O3 fill:#FFB6C1

fields.title: LightGBM Parallel Training Under the Hood
fields.description: Understand how LightGBM achieves fast parallel training with histograms and distributed algorithms

steps.title: The Challenge: Finding Best Splits
steps.explanation: The bottleneck in tree training is finding the **best split point** for each feature. Naive approach: sort all values and try each as threshold. With millions of samples and hundreds of features, this is extremely slow.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Naive Split Finding" D["1M samples × 100 features"] S["Sort each feature: O(n log n)"] T["Try each value as split"] C["Calculate gain for each"] end D --> S --> T --> C C --> SLOW["SLOW!
~100M operations per node"] style SLOW fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: LightGBM's Secret: Histogram-Based Splitting
steps.explanation: Instead of using exact values, LightGBM **buckets** continuous features into discrete bins (default: 255). This reduces split candidates from millions to just 255 per feature, enabling massive speedup.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Histogram Approach"
    V["Feature values: 0.123, 0.456, 0.789, ..."]
    B["Bucket into 255 bins"]
    H["Histogram: count per bin"]
    end
    V --> B --> H
    subgraph "Benefits"
    B1["Only 255 split candidates"]
    B2["O(n) to build histogram"]
    B3["O(bins) to find best split"]
    end
    H --> B1
    H --> B2
    H --> B3
    style B1 fill:#90EE90
    style B2 fill:#90EE90
    style B3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Building Feature Histograms
steps.explanation: For each node, LightGBM builds a **histogram** for every feature. Each bin stores: (1) sum of gradients, (2) sum of hessians, (3) count of samples. These stats enable instant gain calculation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Feature X1 Histogram (8 bins shown)" B1["Bin 0
grad: 2.1
hess: 15
n: 120"] B2["Bin 1
grad: -0.8
hess: 22
n: 180"] B3["Bin 2
grad: 1.5
hess: 18
n: 145"] B4["..."] B5["Bin 254
grad: -1.2
hess: 8
n: 55"] end B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 style B1 fill:#ADD8E6 style B2 fill:#ADD8E6 style B3 fill:#ADD8E6 style B5 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Histogram Subtraction Trick
steps.explanation: **Key optimization**: When splitting a node, the child's histogram = parent's histogram - sibling's histogram. Only compute histogram for the **smaller** child, get the other for free! Cuts work in half.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Parent["Parent Histogram
(already computed)"] Parent --> Left["Left Child
300 samples
COMPUTE"] Parent --> Right["Right Child
700 samples
FREE!"] Calc["Right = Parent - Left"] Left --> Calc Parent --> Calc Calc --> Right style Left fill:#FFFACD style Right fill:#90EE90 style Calc fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Parallel Training Overview
steps.explanation: LightGBM supports multiple parallelism strategies: **Feature Parallel** (split features across workers), **Data Parallel** (split data across workers), and **Voting Parallel** (LightGBM's innovation for large data).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Parallelism Strategies" FP["Feature Parallel
Each worker: subset of features
Best for: few features, many samples"] DP["Data Parallel
Each worker: subset of data
Best for: many features"] VP["Voting Parallel
Hybrid approach
Best for: huge datasets"] end style FP fill:#ADD8E6 style DP fill:#90EE90 style VP fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Feature Parallel: How It Works
steps.explanation: Each worker holds **all data** but only a **subset of features**. Workers build histograms for their features in parallel, then communicate to find the global best split.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Worker 1" W1D["All 1M samples"] W1F["Features 1-25"] W1H["Build histograms
Find local best split"] end subgraph "Worker 2" W2D["All 1M samples"] W2F["Features 26-50"] W2H["Build histograms
Find local best split"] end subgraph "Worker 3" W3D["All 1M samples"] W3F["Features 51-75"] W3H["Build histograms
Find local best split"] end W1H --> SYNC["Sync: Find global best"] W2H --> SYNC W3H --> SYNC SYNC --> SPLIT["Apply same split on all workers"] style SYNC fill:#FFB6C1 style SPLIT fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Feature Parallel: Communication
steps.explanation: After each worker finds its local best split, they exchange split info (feature, threshold, gain). Then **all workers apply the same split** to keep data synchronized. Communication cost: O(workers).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Step 1: Local Best" W1["W1: Feature 12
gain=45.2"] W2["W2: Feature 38
gain=52.1"] W3["W3: Feature 67
gain=41.8"] end subgraph "Step 2: Reduce" ALL["All-reduce:
Best = Feature 38
gain=52.1"] end subgraph "Step 3: Apply" APPLY["All workers split
on Feature 38"] end W1 --> ALL W2 --> ALL W3 --> ALL ALL --> APPLY style W2 fill:#90EE90 style ALL fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Data Parallel: How It Works
steps.explanation: Each worker holds **all features** but only a **subset of data**. Workers build local histograms, then **merge histograms** across workers to get global statistics for split finding.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Worker 1" W1D["Samples 1-333K"] W1F["All 100 features"] W1H["Local histograms"] end subgraph "Worker 2" W2D["Samples 333K-666K"] W2F["All 100 features"] W2H["Local histograms"] end subgraph "Worker 3" W3D["Samples 666K-1M"] W3F["All 100 features"] W3H["Local histograms"] end W1H --> MERGE["Merge histograms
(sum gradients, hessians)"] W2H --> MERGE W3H --> MERGE MERGE --> GLOBAL["Global histogram
Find best split"] style MERGE fill:#FFB6C1 style GLOBAL fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Data Parallel: Reduce-Scatter
steps.explanation: LightGBM uses **Reduce-Scatter** for efficient histogram merging. Each worker ends up with the merged histogram for a **portion of features**, reducing memory and enabling parallel split finding.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Before Reduce-Scatter" W1B["W1: hist(F1-F100) local"] W2B["W2: hist(F1-F100) local"] W3B["W3: hist(F1-F100) local"] end subgraph "Reduce-Scatter" RS["Each worker sends parts
and receives merged parts"] end subgraph "After Reduce-Scatter" W1A["W1: hist(F1-F33) GLOBAL"] W2A["W2: hist(F34-F66) GLOBAL"] W3A["W3: hist(F67-F100) GLOBAL"] end W1B --> RS W2B --> RS W3B --> RS RS --> W1A RS --> W2A RS --> W3A style RS fill:#E6E6FA style W1A fill:#90EE90 style W2A fill:#90EE90 style W3A fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Voting Parallel: LightGBM's Innovation
steps.explanation: For **huge datasets**, communicating full histograms is expensive. Voting Parallel: each worker votes for its **top-k split candidates**. Only candidates with enough votes get full histogram merge. Reduces communication dramatically.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Step 1: Local Top-K" W1["W1 votes:
F12, F38, F45"] W2["W2 votes:
F38, F67, F12"] W3["W3 votes:
F38, F12, F89"] end subgraph "Step 2: Aggregate Votes" V["F38: 3 votes
F12: 3 votes
F45: 1 vote
F67: 1 vote
F89: 1 vote"] end subgraph "Step 3: Merge Winners Only" M["Only merge histograms
for F38 and F12"] end W1 --> V W2 --> V W3 --> V V --> M style V fill:#FFFACD style M fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Communication Comparison
steps.explanation: Different strategies have different communication costs. **Voting Parallel** is most efficient for large-scale distributed training where network bandwidth is the bottleneck.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Communication Cost per Split" FP["Feature Parallel
O(features × bins)
Send histograms for best splits"] DP["Data Parallel
O(features × bins × workers)
Merge all histograms"] VP["Voting Parallel
O(top_k × workers)
Only vote counts + winners"] end FP --> R1["Good for:
Few features"] DP --> R2["Good for:
Many features, fast network"] VP --> R3["Good for:
Huge data, slow network"] style FP fill:#ADD8E6 style DP fill:#90EE90 style VP fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: GPU Parallelism
steps.explanation: LightGBM also supports **GPU training**. The GPU builds histograms in parallel across thousands of CUDA cores. Histogram bins map perfectly to GPU's SIMD architecture.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "GPU Histogram Building" DATA["Training data on GPU memory"] KERN["CUDA Kernel: parallel bin counting"] CORES["1000s of cores process
different data chunks"] HIST["Histograms built in parallel"] end DATA --> KERN --> CORES --> HIST subgraph "Speedup" S1["CPU: 10 seconds"] S2["GPU: 0.5 seconds"] S3["20x faster!"] end HIST --> S3 style CORES fill:#90EE90 style S3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: End-to-End Parallel Training Flow
steps.explanation: Putting it all together: data is distributed, histograms are built in parallel, merged efficiently, best split is found and applied, then repeat for next node. The tree grows across all workers simultaneously.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Distributed Training Loop" A["1. Distribute data to workers"] --> B["2. Build local histograms
(parallel on each worker)"] B --> C["3. Merge/vote for splits
(communication)"] C --> D["4. Find global best split"] D --> E["5. All workers apply split"] E --> F{"More leaves
needed?"} F -->|Yes| B F -->|No| G["6. Tree complete!"] end G --> H["Repeat for next tree
(new residuals)"] style B fill:#90EE90 style C fill:#FFB6C1 style G fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Key Parameters for Parallel Training
steps.explanation: Control parallel behavior with: **num_threads** (CPU threads), **device_type** (cpu/gpu/cuda), **tree_learner** (serial/feature/data/voting), **max_bin** (histogram resolution vs speed tradeoff).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Parallelism Parameters" P1["num_threads=8
CPU threads per worker"] P2["device_type='gpu'"
Enable GPU training"] P3["tree_learner='voting'"
Choose parallel strategy"] P4["max_bin=255
Histogram resolution"] end subgraph "Recommendations" R1["Single machine:
num_threads=-1 (auto)"] R2["Large data:
device_type='gpu'"] R3["Distributed:
tree_learner='voting'"] R4["Speed vs accuracy:
max_bin=63 to 255"] end P1 --> R1 P2 --> R2 P3 --> R3 P4 --> R4 style R1 fill:#90EE90 style R2 fill:#90EE90 style R3 fill:#90EE90 style R4 fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: Random Forest: Complete Guide
fields.description: Understand how Random Forest works from training to prediction, including bagging, feature sampling, and ensemble voting

steps.title: The Big Picture: Ensemble of Trees
steps.explanation: Random Forest is an **ensemble method** that builds many decision trees and combines their predictions. Each tree is trained on a different random subset of data and features, making the forest robust and accurate.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    DATA["Training Data"]
    DATA --> T1["Tree 1"]
    DATA --> T2["Tree 2"]
    DATA --> T3["Tree 3"]
    DATA --> TN["Tree N"]
    T1 --> VOTE["Combine Predictions"]
    T2 --> VOTE
    T3 --> VOTE
    TN --> VOTE
    VOTE --> FINAL["Final Prediction"]
    style VOTE fill:#90EE90
    style FINAL fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Why Multiple Trees? The Wisdom of Crowds
steps.explanation: A single decision tree is prone to **overfitting** - it memorizes training data noise. By training many diverse trees and averaging their predictions, errors cancel out. This is called **bagging** (Bootstrap AGGregatING).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Single Tree Problem" ST["One Tree"] ST --> OF["Overfits to noise
High variance"] end subgraph "Random Forest Solution" RF["100 Trees"] RF --> AVG["Average predictions"] AVG --> STABLE["Stable & accurate
Low variance"] end style OF fill:#FFB6C1 style STABLE fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 1: Bootstrap Sampling
steps.explanation: For each tree, create a **bootstrap sample**: randomly select N samples **with replacement** from the original N samples. Some samples appear multiple times, others not at all (~37% are left out - these become the OOB set).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Original Data (N=6)" D["[A, B, C, D, E, F]"] end subgraph "Bootstrap Samples (with replacement)" B1["Tree 1: [A, A, C, D, D, F]"] B2["Tree 2: [B, B, C, E, E, F]"] B3["Tree 3: [A, C, D, D, E, F]"] end D -->|"Random sample
with replacement"| B1 D -->|"Random sample
with replacement"| B2 D -->|"Random sample
with replacement"| B3 style B1 fill:#ADD8E6 style B2 fill:#90EE90 style B3 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Step 2: Feature Subsampling at Each Split
steps.explanation: When finding the best split at each node, only consider a **random subset of features** (typically √p for classification, p/3 for regression). This decorrelates trees and increases diversity.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "All Features (p=9)" ALL["F1, F2, F3, F4, F5, F6, F7, F8, F9"] end subgraph "At Node Split (√9 = 3 features)" N1["Node 1 considers:
F2, F5, F8"] N2["Node 2 considers:
F1, F4, F7"] N3["Node 3 considers:
F3, F6, F9"] end ALL -->|"Random √p features"| N1 ALL -->|"Random √p features"| N2 ALL -->|"Random √p features"| N3 N1 --> BEST1["Best: F5 < 0.3"] N2 --> BEST2["Best: F4 < 0.7"] style BEST1 fill:#90EE90 style BEST2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Building a Single Tree
steps.explanation: Each tree grows using its bootstrap sample. At every node: (1) randomly select m features, (2) find the best split among those m, (3) split the node, (4) repeat recursively until stopping criteria met.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Tree Building Algorithm" A["Start: All bootstrap samples at root"] B["Select random m features"] C["Find best split among m"] D["Split node into two children"] E{"Stopping criteria?"} F["Create leaf with prediction"] end A --> B --> C --> D --> E E -->|"No: min_samples,
max_depth not reached"| B E -->|"Yes"| F style F fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Tree 1: Example Structure
steps.explanation: Here's what Tree 1 might look like after training on its bootstrap sample. Notice it uses different features at different nodes. Trees are typically grown deep (low bias) since variance is reduced by averaging.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB R1["F5 < 0.3?"] R1 -->|Yes| L1["F2 < 0.6?"] R1 -->|No| L2["F8 < 0.4?"] L1 -->|Yes| LL1["Predict: A
samples: 45"] L1 -->|No| LR1["F9 < 0.5?"] LR1 -->|Yes| LRL["Predict: B
samples: 28"] LR1 -->|No| LRR["Predict: A
samples: 12"] L2 -->|Yes| RL1["Predict: B
samples: 67"] L2 -->|No| RR1["Predict: C
samples: 23"] style R1 fill:#E6E6FA style LL1 fill:#ADD8E6 style LRL fill:#90EE90 style LRR fill:#ADD8E6 style RL1 fill:#90EE90 style RR1 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Tree 2: Different Structure
steps.explanation: Tree 2 trained on a different bootstrap sample and used different random features at splits. It has a completely different structure! This **diversity** is key to Random Forest's power.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB R2["F1 < 0.7?"] R2 -->|Yes| L3["F4 < 0.2?"] R2 -->|No| L4["F6 < 0.8?"] L3 -->|Yes| LL2["Predict: B
samples: 38"] L3 -->|No| LR2["Predict: A
samples: 51"] L4 -->|Yes| RL2["F3 < 0.5?"] L4 -->|No| RR2["Predict: C
samples: 31"] RL2 -->|Yes| RLL["Predict: B
samples: 29"] RL2 -->|No| RLR["Predict: A
samples: 26"] style R2 fill:#E6E6FA style LL2 fill:#90EE90 style LR2 fill:#ADD8E6 style RR2 fill:#FFFACD style RLL fill:#90EE90 style RLR fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: The Complete Forest
steps.explanation: After training, we have N trees (typically 100-500), each with unique structure due to bootstrap sampling and feature randomization. Together they form a powerful ensemble.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Random Forest (N=100 trees)" T1["Tree 1
Bootstrap 1
Depth: 12"] T2["Tree 2
Bootstrap 2
Depth: 15"] T3["Tree 3
Bootstrap 3
Depth: 11"] TD["..."] T100["Tree 100
Bootstrap 100
Depth: 14"] end NOTE["Each tree is different due to:
1. Different bootstrap samples
2. Different random features at splits"] style T1 fill:#ADD8E6 style T2 fill:#90EE90 style T3 fill:#FFFACD style T100 fill:#FFB6C1 style NOTE fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Making Predictions: Classification
steps.explanation: For **classification**: pass the sample through ALL trees, collect each tree's vote, and use **majority voting**. The class with the most votes wins. Probability = proportion of trees voting for each class.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB NEW["New Sample X"] NEW --> T1["Tree 1: Class A"] NEW --> T2["Tree 2: Class B"] NEW --> T3["Tree 3: Class A"] NEW --> T4["Tree 4: Class A"] NEW --> T5["Tree 5: Class B"] T1 --> VOTE["Vote Count:
A: 3 votes (60%)
B: 2 votes (40%)"] T2 --> VOTE T3 --> VOTE T4 --> VOTE T5 --> VOTE VOTE --> PRED["Prediction: Class A
Probability: 0.60"] style PRED fill:#90EE90 style T1 fill:#ADD8E6 style T3 fill:#ADD8E6 style T4 fill:#ADD8E6 style T2 fill:#FFB6C1 style T5 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Making Predictions: Regression
steps.explanation: For **regression**: pass the sample through all trees, collect each tree's numeric prediction, and compute the **average**. This averaging smooths out individual tree errors.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB NEW["New Sample X"] NEW --> T1["Tree 1: 23.5"] NEW --> T2["Tree 2: 25.1"] NEW --> T3["Tree 3: 22.8"] NEW --> T4["Tree 4: 24.2"] NEW --> T5["Tree 5: 23.9"] T1 --> AVG["Average:
(23.5+25.1+22.8+24.2+23.9)/5"] T2 --> AVG T3 --> AVG T4 --> AVG T5 --> AVG AVG --> PRED["Prediction: 23.9"] style PRED fill:#90EE90 style AVG fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Out-of-Bag (OOB) Error Estimation
steps.explanation: ~37% of samples are left out of each bootstrap sample. For each sample, predict using only trees where it was **not** in the training set. This gives a free validation estimate without needing a separate test set!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Sample A" SA["In: Tree 1, Tree 3
Out: Tree 2, Tree 4, Tree 5"] end subgraph "OOB Prediction for A" OOB["Use only Tree 2, 4, 5
(trees that didn't see A)"] PRED["OOB Prediction for A"] end SA --> OOB --> PRED subgraph "OOB Score" ALL["Do this for all samples"] SCORE["OOB Accuracy ≈ Test Accuracy"] end PRED --> ALL --> SCORE style SCORE fill:#90EE90 style OOB fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Feature Importance: Gini Importance
steps.explanation: **Gini importance** (or Mean Decrease in Impurity): sum up how much each feature reduces impurity across all trees and all splits. Features that create purer splits are more important.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Across All Trees" T1S["Tree 1: F5 reduces Gini by 0.15"] T2S["Tree 2: F5 reduces Gini by 0.12"] T3S["Tree 3: F5 reduces Gini by 0.18"] end T1S --> SUM["Sum for F5:
0.15 + 0.12 + 0.18 = 0.45"] T2S --> SUM T3S --> SUM SUM --> NORM["Normalize across features"] NORM --> IMP["F5 Importance: 0.23
F1 Importance: 0.18
F3 Importance: 0.15
..."] style IMP fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Feature Importance: Permutation Importance
steps.explanation: **Permutation importance**: shuffle one feature's values and measure how much accuracy drops. Large drop = important feature. More reliable than Gini importance for correlated features.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Original"
    O["OOB Accuracy: 0.92"]
    end
    subgraph "Shuffle Feature F5"
    S["Shuffle F5 values randomly"]
    P["New OOB Accuracy: 0.78"]
    end
    subgraph "Importance"
    I["F5 Importance = 0.92 - 0.78 = 0.14"]
    end
    O --> S --> P --> I
    R["Repeat for each feature"]
    I --> R
    style I fill:#90EE90
    style P fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Key Hyperparameters
steps.explanation: Important parameters to tune: **n_estimators** (number of trees), **max_features** (features per split), **max_depth** (tree depth), **min_samples_split**, **min_samples_leaf**, **bootstrap** (True/False).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Number of Trees" N["n_estimators=100-500
More trees = better but slower
Diminishing returns after ~100"] end subgraph "Features per Split" M["max_features='sqrt' (classification)
max_features='auto' (regression)
Lower = more diversity"] end subgraph "Tree Depth" D["max_depth=None (grow fully)
Deeper = lower bias
Averaging handles variance"] end subgraph "Leaf Size" L["min_samples_leaf=1
Higher = more regularization"] end style N fill:#ADD8E6 style M fill:#90EE90 style D fill:#FFFACD style L fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Parallelization
steps.explanation: Random Forest is **embarrassingly parallel**: trees are independent and can be trained simultaneously. Set **n_jobs=-1** to use all CPU cores. Training and prediction both parallelize well.

steps.title: Random Forest vs Single Decision Tree
steps.explanation: Comparison: Single trees are fast and interpretable but overfit. Random Forests are slower and less interpretable but much more accurate and robust. The ensemble trades simplicity for performance.

steps.title: scikit-learn Implementation
steps.explanation: Using Random Forest in scikit-learn is straightforward. Import the classifier or regressor, fit on training data, predict, and access feature importances and OOB score.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code Flow" C1["from sklearn.ensemble import
RandomForestClassifier"] C2["rf = RandomForestClassifier(
n_estimators=100,
max_features='sqrt',
oob_score=True,
n_jobs=-1)"] C3["rf.fit(X_train, y_train)"] C4["predictions = rf.predict(X_test)"] C5["probabilities = rf.predict_proba(X_test)"] C6["importance = rf.feature_importances_"] C7["oob = rf.oob_score_"] end C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 C3 --> C5 C3 --> C6 C3 --> C7 style C4 fill:#90EE90 style C5 fill:#ADD8E6 style C6 fill:#FFFACD style C7 fill:#FFB6C1

fields.title: ExtraTrees: Extremely Randomized Trees
fields.description: Learn how ExtraTrees adds even more randomization than Random Forest for faster training and lower variance

steps.title: ExtraTrees: Even More Random Than Random Forest
steps.explanation: **Extremely Randomized Trees (ExtraTrees)** takes the Random Forest concept further: instead of finding the *best* split among random features, it uses *random* split thresholds. This extra randomness trades a bit more bias for much lower variance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Random Forest" RF1["Random features"] RF2["BEST split threshold"] end subgraph "ExtraTrees" ET1["Random features"] ET2["RANDOM split threshold"] end RF2 --> MORE["More randomness"] ET2 --> MORE MORE --> BENEFIT["Lower variance
Faster training"] style RF2 fill:#FFFACD style ET2 fill:#90EE90 style BENEFIT fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Key Difference #1: No Bootstrap Sampling
steps.explanation: By default, ExtraTrees uses the **entire dataset** for each tree (bootstrap=False). Random Forest samples with replacement. Using all data gives each tree more information but less diversity from sampling.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB DATA["Original Data (N samples)"] subgraph "Random Forest" RF["Bootstrap sample
N samples WITH replacement
~63% unique samples"] end subgraph "ExtraTrees (default)" ET["Use ALL N samples
No sampling
100% of data"] end DATA --> RF DATA --> ET style RF fill:#FFFACD style ET fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Key Difference #2: Random Split Thresholds
steps.explanation: This is the **main innovation**. When splitting a node: Random Forest tries all possible thresholds to find the best. ExtraTrees picks ONE random threshold per feature and uses the best among those. Much faster!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Random Forest Split Finding" RF1["Feature X1: values [0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9]"] RF2["Try ALL thresholds:
0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8"] RF3["Calculate gain for each"] RF4["Pick best: 0.4 (gain=0.35)"] end subgraph "ExtraTrees Split Finding" ET1["Feature X1: values [0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9]"] ET2["Pick ONE random threshold:
0.6"] ET3["Calculate gain: 0.28"] ET4["Done! Use 0.6"] end RF1 --> RF2 --> RF3 --> RF4 ET1 --> ET2 --> ET3 --> ET4 style RF3 fill:#FFB6C1 style ET2 fill:#90EE90 style ET4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: How ExtraTrees Finds a Split
steps.explanation: At each node: (1) Select random subset of features (like RF), (2) For EACH selected feature, pick ONE random threshold between min and max, (3) Evaluate gain for each, (4) Use the feature+threshold with best gain.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB NODE["Node with 500 samples"] NODE --> SELECT["1. Select √p random features
e.g., F2, F5, F8"] SELECT --> RAND["2. Random threshold per feature"] subgraph "Random Thresholds" R1["F2: range [0,10]
Random: 4.7"] R2["F5: range [0,1]
Random: 0.33"] R3["F8: range [-5,5]
Random: 1.2"] end RAND --> R1 RAND --> R2 RAND --> R3 R1 --> EVAL["3. Evaluate gains"] R2 --> EVAL R3 --> EVAL EVAL --> BEST["4. Best: F5 < 0.33
gain = 0.42"] style BEST fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Why Random Thresholds Work
steps.explanation: Intuition: with enough trees, random thresholds will eventually "find" good splits. Individual trees may be weaker, but the ensemble averages out the randomness. More trees compensate for suboptimal individual splits.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Single Tree" ST["Random thresholds
= suboptimal splits
= weaker tree"] end subgraph "Many Trees (100+)" MT["Random variations
cancel out"] AVG["Average predictions"] RESULT["Strong ensemble
despite weak individuals"] end ST --> MT --> AVG --> RESULT style ST fill:#FFB6C1 style RESULT fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Training Speed Comparison
steps.explanation: ExtraTrees is **significantly faster** to train. Finding the optimal split requires sorting or histogram computation. Random thresholds skip this entirely - just pick a number and evaluate once!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Random Forest" RF1["For each feature:"] RF2["Sort values or
build histogram"] RF3["Try O(n) thresholds"] RF4["O(n × features)"] end subgraph "ExtraTrees" ET1["For each feature:"] ET2["Pick random number"] ET3["Evaluate once"] ET4["O(features)"] end RF4 --> SLOW["Slower"] ET4 --> FAST["Much Faster!"] style SLOW fill:#FFB6C1 style FAST fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Bias-Variance Trade-off
steps.explanation: ExtraTrees has **higher bias** (suboptimal splits) but **lower variance** (more randomization smooths predictions). For many problems, especially noisy data, this trade-off is favorable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Random Forest" RFB["Lower Bias
(optimal splits)"] RFV["Higher Variance
(less randomization)"] end subgraph "ExtraTrees" ETB["Higher Bias
(random splits)"] ETV["Lower Variance
(extreme randomization)"] end subgraph "When ExtraTrees Wins" W1["Noisy data"] W2["Risk of overfitting"] W3["Need fast training"] end style RFB fill:#90EE90 style RFV fill:#FFB6C1 style ETB fill:#FFFACD style ETV fill:#90EE90 style W1 fill:#ADD8E6 style W2 fill:#ADD8E6 style W3 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Making Predictions
