steps.explanation: In practice, frameworks handle gradient descent. In **scikit-learn**, use `SGDClassifier`/`SGDRegressor`. In **PyTorch/TensorFlow**, use optimizers like `Adam` or `SGD`. Just define model and loss, call `optimizer.step()`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Scikit-learn" S1["from sklearn.linear_model
import SGDClassifier"] S2["clf = SGDClassifier(
learning_rate='adaptive')"] S3["clf.fit(X, y)"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 end subgraph "PyTorch" P1["optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(
model.parameters(), lr=0.001)"] P2["loss.backward() # compute gradient"] P3["optimizer.step() # update weights"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 end style S2 fill:#ADD8E6 style P1 fill:#E6E6FA

fields.title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
fields.description: Understand the fundamental tradeoff in machine learning: what bias and variance mean, how they relate to underfitting/overfitting, and strategies to find the optimal model complexity.

steps.title: What is Prediction Error?
steps.explanation: When a model makes predictions, it has **error** - the difference between predicted and actual values. This error can be decomposed into three components: **Bias**, **Variance**, and **Irreducible Noise**. Understanding this helps us build better models.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Error["Total Prediction Error"] Error --> Bias["Bias²"] Error --> Var["Variance"] Error --> Noise["Irreducible Noise"] Formula["Error = Bias² + Variance + Noise"] Bias --> Control["We can control"] Var --> Control Noise --> Cannot["Cannot reduce
(inherent randomness)"] style Formula fill:#FFFACD style Control fill:#90EE90 style Cannot fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: What is Bias?
steps.explanation: **Bias** measures how far off our model's average prediction is from the true value. High bias means the model makes **systematic errors** - it consistently misses in the same direction. This happens when the model is too simple to capture the true pattern.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Bias["Bias"] Bias --> Def["Difference between
average prediction
and true value"] Bias --> High["High Bias"] Bias --> Low["Low Bias"] High --> H1["Model too simple"] High --> H2["Underfitting"] High --> H3["Misses patterns"] Low --> L1["Model captures
true relationship"] style High fill:#FFB6C1 style Low fill:#90EE90

steps.title: What is Variance?
steps.explanation: **Variance** measures how much predictions change when trained on different datasets. High variance means the model is **sensitive to training data** - small changes in data cause big changes in predictions. This happens when the model is too complex.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Var["Variance"] Var --> Def["How much predictions
vary across different
training sets"] Var --> High["High Variance"] Var --> Low["Low Variance"] High --> H1["Model too complex"] High --> H2["Overfitting"] High --> H3["Learns noise"] Low --> L1["Stable predictions
across datasets"] style High fill:#FFB6C1 style Low fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The Bullseye Analogy
steps.explanation: Imagine shooting arrows at a target. **Bias** = how far the average shot is from center. **Variance** = how spread out the shots are. We want low bias (centered) AND low variance (tight cluster).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Low Bias, Low Variance" A["🎯 Clustered
at center"] A --> Best["✓ IDEAL"] end subgraph "Low Bias, High Variance" B["Scattered
around center"] B --> LV["Average is right
but inconsistent"] end subgraph "High Bias, Low Variance" C["Clustered
off-center"] C --> HB["Consistently
wrong"] end subgraph "High Bias, High Variance" D["Scattered
off-center"] D --> Worst["✗ WORST"] end style Best fill:#90EE90 style Worst fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: The Tradeoff
steps.explanation: Here's the catch: reducing bias often **increases variance**, and vice versa. Simple models → high bias, low variance. Complex models → low bias, high variance. We need to find the **sweet spot** that minimizes total error.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Simple["Simple Model
(few parameters)"] Complex["Complex Model
(many parameters)"] Simple --> HB["High Bias"] Simple --> LV["Low Variance"] Complex --> LB["Low Bias"] Complex --> HV["High Variance"] HB --> Under["Underfitting"] HV --> Over["Overfitting"] Sweet["Sweet Spot
in the middle!"] style Under fill:#FFB6C1 style Over fill:#FFB6C1 style Sweet fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Model Complexity Curve
steps.explanation: As model complexity increases: bias decreases (better fit), variance increases (more sensitive). **Total error** is U-shaped - too simple or too complex both give high error. The minimum is our target complexity.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Error vs Complexity" Low["Low Complexity"] Med["Optimal
Complexity"] High["High Complexity"] Low --> |"High bias
dominates"| LE["High Error"] Med --> |"Balanced"| ME["Minimum Error"] High --> |"High variance
dominates"| HE["High Error"] end Train["Training error
always decreases"] Test["Test error
U-shaped curve"] style ME fill:#90EE90 style LE fill:#FFB6C1 style HE fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Underfitting (High Bias)
steps.explanation: **Underfitting** occurs when the model is too simple. Signs: high training error AND high test error. The model can't even fit the training data. Fix: add features, use more complex model, reduce regularization.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Under["Underfitting"]
    Under --> Signs["Signs"]
    Signs --> S1["High training error"]
    Signs --> S2["High test error"]
    Signs --> S3["Both errors similar"]
    Under --> Fix["Fixes"]
    Fix --> F1["Add more features"]
    Fix --> F2["Use complex model"]
    Fix --> F3["Reduce regularization"]
    Fix --> F4["Train longer"]
    style Under fill:#FFB6C1
    style Fix fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Overfitting (High Variance)
steps.explanation: **Overfitting** occurs when the model is too complex. Signs: low training error but high test error (big gap). The model memorizes training data including noise. Fix: more data, regularization, simpler model, dropout.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Over["Overfitting"] Over --> Signs["Signs"] Signs --> S1["Low training error"] Signs --> S2["High test error"] Signs --> S3["Large gap between them"] Over --> Fix["Fixes"] Fix --> F1["Get more data"] Fix --> F2["Add regularization
(L1, L2, dropout)"] Fix --> F3["Simpler model"] Fix --> F4["Early stopping"] style Over fill:#FFB6C1 style Fix fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Diagnosing with Learning Curves
steps.explanation: **Learning curves** plot error vs training set size. They reveal whether you have a bias or variance problem. High bias: curves plateau high. High variance: large gap between train/test that shrinks with more data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "High Bias" HB1["Train & test error
both high"] HB2["Curves plateau
close together"] HB3["More data won't help!"] HB1 --> HB2 --> HB3 end subgraph "High Variance" HV1["Training error low"] HV2["Test error much higher"] HV3["Gap shrinks with
more data"] HV1 --> HV2 --> HV3 end style HB3 fill:#FFB6C1 style HV3 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Regularization Reduces Variance
steps.explanation: **Regularization** constrains model complexity by penalizing large weights. This reduces variance at the cost of slightly increased bias. Common techniques: L1 (Lasso), L2 (Ridge), Dropout, Early Stopping.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Reg["Regularization"] Reg --> L1["L1 (Lasso)
Sparse weights"] Reg --> L2["L2 (Ridge)
Small weights"] Reg --> Drop["Dropout
Random neurons off"] Reg --> Early["Early Stopping
Stop before overfit"] Effect["Effect: ↑ Bias slightly
↓ Variance significantly"] L1 --> Effect L2 --> Effect style Reg fill:#ADD8E6 style Effect fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Ensemble Methods Reduce Variance
steps.explanation: **Ensemble methods** combine multiple models to reduce variance. **Bagging** (Random Forest) trains models on different data subsets - averaging reduces variance. **Boosting** (XGBoost) reduces bias by fixing errors iteratively.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Ensemble["Ensemble Methods"] Ensemble --> Bag["Bagging
(Random Forest)"] Ensemble --> Boost["Boosting
(XGBoost, AdaBoost)"] Bag --> B1["Train on different
data samples"] Bag --> B2["Average predictions"] Bag --> B3["Reduces VARIANCE"] Boost --> Bo1["Train sequentially"] Boost --> Bo2["Focus on errors"] Boost --> Bo3["Reduces BIAS"] style B3 fill:#90EE90 style Bo3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Summary: Finding the Balance
steps.explanation: The goal is to find the optimal complexity that minimizes **total error**. Use cross-validation to estimate test error. Watch for signs of underfitting (both errors high) or overfitting (train low, test high). Adjust complexity accordingly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Start["Evaluate Model"] Start --> Check{"Train & Test
Error?"} Check --> |"Both high"| Under["Underfitting
↑ Complexity"] Check --> |"Train low
Test high"| Over["Overfitting
↓ Complexity"] Check --> |"Both low
& close"| Good["Good Fit! ✓"] Under --> Try1["Add features
Complex model"] Over --> Try2["Regularize
More data"] style Under fill:#FFB6C1 style Over fill:#FFB6C1 style Good fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Spark DAG Execution
fields.description: Understand how Apache Spark executes your code: DAG construction, lazy evaluation, stages, shuffles, and optimization. Learn to read execution plans and improve performance.

steps.title: What is a DAG?
steps.explanation: A **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** represents the sequence of computations Spark will perform. Each node is an RDD/DataFrame operation, edges show data flow. 'Acyclic' means no loops - data flows one direction. Spark builds this plan before executing anything.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR A["Read CSV"] --> B["Filter"] B --> C["Map"] C --> D["GroupBy"] D --> E["Aggregate"] E --> F["Write"] DAG["DAG = Execution Plan
Built BEFORE running"] style DAG fill:#FFFACD style A fill:#ADD8E6 style F fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Lazy Evaluation
steps.explanation: Spark uses **lazy evaluation**: transformations (filter, map, join) don't execute immediately. They just build the DAG. Only when an **action** (collect, count, write) is called does Spark execute the entire plan. This enables optimization!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Transformations (Lazy)" T1["df.filter()"] --> T2["df.select()"] T2 --> T3["df.groupBy()"] T3 --> Note1["Nothing executed yet!
Just building DAG"] end subgraph "Action (Triggers Execution)" A1["df.count()"] A1 --> Exec["NOW Spark executes
the entire DAG"] end T3 --> A1 style Note1 fill:#FFFACD style Exec fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Transformations vs Actions
steps.explanation: **Transformations** create new RDDs/DataFrames (lazy). **Actions** return results or write data (trigger execution). Know the difference - it's fundamental to understanding Spark performance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Transformations (Lazy)"
        T1["map, flatMap"]
        T2["filter, where"]
        T3["select, withColumn"]
        T4["groupBy, join"]
        T5["orderBy, distinct"]
    end
    subgraph "Actions (Execute)"
        A1["collect, take"]
        A2["count, first"]
        A3["show, head"]
        A4["write, save"]
        A5["reduce, foreach"]
    end
    T1 --> Build["Build DAG"]
    T5 --> Build
    A1 --> Run["Execute DAG"]
    A5 --> Run
    style Build fill:#ADD8E6
    style Run fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Narrow vs Wide Transformations
steps.explanation: **Narrow transformations** (map, filter): each input partition contributes to ONE output partition - no data movement. **Wide transformations** (groupBy, join): data must **shuffle** across partitions - expensive! Wide transformations create stage boundaries.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Narrow (No Shuffle)" N1["Partition 1"] --> N1out["Partition 1"] N2["Partition 2"] --> N2out["Partition 2"] N3["Partition 3"] --> N3out["Partition 3"] end subgraph "Wide (Shuffle Required)" W1["Partition 1"] --> WA["Partition A"] W1 --> WB["Partition B"] W2["Partition 2"] --> WA W2 --> WB W3["Partition 3"] --> WA W3 --> WB end Narrow["filter, map, select
✓ Fast, no network"] Wide["groupBy, join, orderBy
✗ Shuffle = expensive"] style Narrow fill:#90EE90 style Wide fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Jobs, Stages, and Tasks
steps.explanation: **Job**: triggered by each action. **Stage**: a set of tasks that can run in parallel (bounded by shuffles). **Task**: smallest unit of work, one task per partition per stage. Shuffles create stage boundaries.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Action["Action (e.g., count)"] Action --> Job["Job"] Job --> Stage1["Stage 1
(before shuffle)"] Job --> Stage2["Stage 2
(after shuffle)"] Stage1 --> T1["Task 1.1
Partition 1"] Stage1 --> T2["Task 1.2
Partition 2"] Stage1 --> T3["Task 1.3
Partition 3"] Stage2 --> T4["Task 2.1"] Stage2 --> T5["Task 2.2"] Shuffle["Shuffle
(Stage Boundary)"] Stage1 --> Shuffle --> Stage2 style Job fill:#ADD8E6 style Shuffle fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: How Stages Are Created
steps.explanation: Spark analyzes the DAG and creates stages by working **backwards** from the action. Each wide transformation (shuffle) creates a new stage. Tasks within a stage run in parallel; stages run sequentially.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Stage 1" A["Read"] --> B["Filter"] B --> C["Map"] end subgraph "Shuffle" S["Data redistributed
across network"] end subgraph "Stage 2" D["GroupBy"] --> E["Agg"] end subgraph "Stage 3" F["Sort"] --> G["Write"] end C --> S --> D E --> S2["Shuffle"] --> F style S fill:#FFB6C1 style S2 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Shuffle: The Expensive Operation
steps.explanation: **Shuffle** moves data across the cluster: data is written to disk, sent over network, and read by other executors. This is the most expensive operation in Spark. Minimize shuffles for better performance!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Executor 1" E1["Map output"] E1 --> Disk1["Write to disk"] end subgraph "Executor 2" E2["Map output"] E2 --> Disk2["Write to disk"] end Disk1 --> Net["Network Transfer"] Disk2 --> Net subgraph "Executor 3" Net --> Read["Read shuffle data"] Read --> Reduce["Reduce"] end Cost["Shuffle Cost:
Disk I/O + Network + Serialization"] style Cost fill:#FFB6C1 style Net fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: DAG Optimization: Catalyst
steps.explanation: Spark's **Catalyst optimizer** analyzes and optimizes the DAG before execution. It applies rules like: predicate pushdown (filter early), projection pruning (select only needed columns), and join reordering. This happens automatically!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Logical["Logical Plan
(What you wrote)"] Logical --> Analyze["Analyze"] Analyze --> Optimize["Optimize"] Optimize --> Physical["Physical Plan
(How Spark runs it)"] subgraph "Optimizations" O1["Predicate Pushdown
Filter early"] O2["Projection Pruning
Drop unused columns"] O3["Join Reordering
Smallest tables first"] end Optimize --> O1 Optimize --> O2 Optimize --> O3 style Optimize fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Example: Word Count DAG
steps.explanation: Let's trace a word count DAG: read → flatMap (split lines) → map (word, 1) → reduceByKey (count). The reduceByKey causes a shuffle, creating two stages.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Stage 1 (Map Side)" A["Read text file"] A --> B["flatMap:
split into words"] B --> C["map:
(word, 1)"] end subgraph "Shuffle" S["Shuffle by key
(word)"] end subgraph "Stage 2 (Reduce Side)" D["reduceByKey:
sum counts"] D --> E["collect()"] end C --> S --> D style S fill:#FFB6C1 style E fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Viewing the DAG: Spark UI
steps.explanation: The **Spark UI** (port 4040) visualizes the DAG. Go to the Jobs tab to see stages; click a stage to see tasks. The DAG Visualization shows the execution plan graphically. Use this to debug performance issues!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB UI["Spark UI
localhost:4040"] UI --> Jobs["Jobs Tab"] UI --> Stages["Stages Tab"] UI --> Storage["Storage Tab"] UI --> SQL["SQL Tab
(for DataFrames)"] Jobs --> J1["Job timeline"] Jobs --> J2["DAG visualization"] Stages --> S1["Task metrics"] Stages --> S2["Shuffle read/write"] SQL --> Plan["Query plan
with optimizations"] style UI fill:#ADD8E6 style Plan fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Caching: Reuse Intermediate Results
steps.explanation: If you use a DataFrame multiple times, **cache()** or **persist()** stores it in memory. Without caching, Spark recomputes from the start each time! Caching breaks the DAG recomputation chain.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Without Cache" A1["Read"] --> B1["Transform"] B1 --> C1["Action 1"] A2["Read"] --> B2["Transform"] B2 --> C2["Action 2"] Note1["Recomputes everything
for each action!"] end subgraph "With Cache" A3["Read"] --> B3["Transform"] B3 --> Cache["cache()"] Cache --> C3["Action 1"] Cache --> C4["Action 2"] Note2["Computed once,
reused from memory"] end style Note1 fill:#FFB6C1 style Note2 fill:#90EE90 style Cache fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Performance Tips
steps.explanation: To optimize DAG execution: minimize shuffles (avoid unnecessary groupBy/join), filter early, select only needed columns, use broadcast joins for small tables, cache reused DataFrames, and check the Spark UI for bottlenecks.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Tips["DAG Performance Tips"] Tips --> T1["Minimize shuffles
Avoid wide transforms"] Tips --> T2["Filter early
Reduce data ASAP"] Tips --> T3["Select few columns
Projection pruning"] Tips --> T4["Broadcast small tables
Avoid shuffle joins"] Tips --> T5["Cache wisely
Reused DataFrames"] Tips --> T6["Check Spark UI
Find bottlenecks"] style Tips fill:#ADD8E6 style T1 fill:#90EE90 style T2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Python Example: Explain Plan
steps.explanation: Use **explain()** to see the physical plan Spark will execute. Add `True` for extended output showing all optimization stages. This helps you understand what Spark will actually do.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "View Execution Plan" C1["df.explain()"] C1 --> Basic["Physical plan"] C2["df.explain(True)"] C2 --> Full["Parsed → Analyzed →
Optimized → Physical"] end subgraph "Key Things to Look For" L1["Scan (data source)"] L2["Exchange (shuffle!)"] L3["BroadcastHashJoin
(good for small tables)"] L4["SortMergeJoin
(shuffle both sides)"] end style C2 fill:#ADD8E6 style L2 fill:#FFB6C1 style L3 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: GroupBy: Split-Apply-Combine
fields.description: Master Pandas groupby operations: understand the split-apply-combine pattern, aggregations, transforms, filters, and the agg() function for powerful data analysis.

steps.title: What is Split-Apply-Combine?
steps.explanation: **Split-Apply-Combine** is a data analysis pattern: **Split** data into groups, **Apply** a function to each group, **Combine** results back together. This is the foundation of `groupby()` in Pandas - one of its most powerful features.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Data["Original
DataFrame"] Data --> Split["SPLIT
by key"] Split --> G1["Group A"] Split --> G2["Group B"] Split --> G3["Group C"] G1 --> Apply1["APPLY
function"] G2 --> Apply2["APPLY
function"] G3 --> Apply3["APPLY
function"] Apply1 --> Combine["COMBINE
results"] Apply2 --> Combine Apply3 --> Combine Combine --> Result["Result"] style Split fill:#ADD8E6 style Apply1 fill:#E6E6FA style Combine fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The GroupBy Object
steps.explanation: Calling `df.groupby('column')` returns a **GroupBy object** - not the result! It's lazy, like Spark. No computation happens until you call an aggregation. Think of it as instructions for how to split the data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Code["df.groupby('city')"] Code --> GBO["GroupBy Object
(lazy - no computation yet)"] GBO --> What["Contains:
• Grouping keys
• References to data
• Aggregation methods"] GBO --> Then["Then call:"] Then --> Agg[".sum(), .mean()
.count(), .agg()"] Agg --> Result["Actual Result"] style GBO fill:#FFFACD style Result fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 1: SPLIT
steps.explanation: The **Split** phase divides the DataFrame into groups based on unique values in the grouping column(s). Each group contains all rows that share the same key value. No data is copied - Pandas uses indices.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Original DataFrame"
        D1["Alice | NYC | 100"]
        D2["Bob | LA | 200"]
        D3["Carol | NYC | 150"]
        D4["Dave | LA | 300"]
    end
    Split["groupby('city')"]
    D1 --> Split
    D2 --> Split
    D3 --> Split
    D4 --> Split
    subgraph "Group: NYC"
        G1["Alice | NYC | 100"]
        G2["Carol | NYC | 150"]
    end
    subgraph "Group: LA"
        G3["Bob | LA | 200"]
        G4["Dave | LA | 300"]
    end
    Split --> G1
    Split --> G2
    Split --> G3
    Split --> G4
    style Split fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Step 2: APPLY
steps.explanation: The **Apply** phase runs a function on each group independently. Common operations: aggregations (sum, mean, count), transformations (normalize, fill), or filters (keep groups meeting criteria).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "NYC Group" N1["100"] N2["150"] N1 --> NSum["sum = 250"] end subgraph "LA Group" L1["200"] L2["300"] L1 --> LSum["sum = 500"] end Apply["Apply: sum()"] NSum --> Apply LSum --> Apply Types["Apply Types:
• Aggregation (reduce)
• Transform (same shape)
• Filter (subset groups)"] style Apply fill:#E6E6FA style Types fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Step 3: COMBINE
steps.explanation: The **Combine** phase assembles results from all groups. For aggregations, you get one row per group. For transforms, you get the same shape as input. The group keys become the index (or a column with `as_index=False`).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Group Results" R1["NYC: 250"] R2["LA: 500"] end Combine["Combine"] R1 --> Combine R2 --> Combine subgraph "Final Result" F1["city | sales"] F2["NYC | 250"] F3["LA | 500"] end Combine --> F1 Index["Group key becomes
index by default"] style Combine fill:#90EE90 style Index fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Common Aggregations
steps.explanation: Pandas provides many built-in aggregation methods. These are optimized and faster than custom functions. Each returns one value per group.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Aggs["Built-in Aggregations"]
    Aggs --> Num["Numeric"]
    Aggs --> Count["Counting"]
    Aggs --> Pos["Position"]
    Num --> N1[".sum()"]
    Num --> N2[".mean()"]
    Num --> N3[".std(), .var()"]
    Num --> N4[".min(), .max()"]
    Count --> C1[".count()"]
    Count --> C2[".nunique()"]
    Count --> C3[".size()"]
    Pos --> P1[".first(), .last()"]
    Pos --> P2[".nth(n)"]
    style Aggs fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Multiple Aggregations: agg()
steps.explanation: Use **agg()** to apply multiple functions at once or different functions to different columns. Pass a dict to specify which function for which column, or a list to apply all functions to all columns.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Same function, multiple columns" A1["df.groupby('city').agg('sum')"] A1 --> R1["Sums all numeric columns"] end subgraph "Multiple functions" A2["df.groupby('city')['sales']
.agg(['sum', 'mean', 'max'])"] A2 --> R2["Three columns per group"] end subgraph "Different per column" A3["df.groupby('city').agg({
'sales': 'sum',
'quantity': 'mean'
})"] A3 --> R3["Custom per column"] end style A2 fill:#ADD8E6 style A3 fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Named Aggregations
steps.explanation: **Named aggregations** (Pandas 0.25+) let you specify output column names clearly. Use `pd.NamedAgg` or tuple syntax. This is the cleanest way to do complex aggregations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Code["df.groupby('city').agg(
total_sales=('sales', 'sum'),
avg_quantity=('qty', 'mean'),
num_orders=('order_id', 'count')
)"] Code --> Result["city | total_sales | avg_quantity | num_orders"] Clean["✓ Clear output names
✓ Multiple columns
✓ Different functions"] style Code fill:#ADD8E6 style Clean fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Transform: Same Shape Output
steps.explanation: **transform()** returns data with the same shape as input - the result is broadcast back to original rows. Perfect for: adding group statistics as columns, normalizing within groups, filling missing values per group.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original" O1["Alice | NYC | 100"] O2["Bob | LA | 200"] O3["Carol | NYC | 150"] end Trans["transform('mean')"] subgraph "Result (same rows)" R1["Alice | NYC | 125"] R2["Bob | LA | 200"] R3["Carol | NYC | 125"] end O1 --> Trans --> R1 O2 --> Trans --> R2 O3 --> Trans --> R3 Note["Each row gets its
GROUP's mean"] style Trans fill:#E6E6FA style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Transform Use Case: Normalize
steps.explanation: A common pattern: normalize values within each group by subtracting group mean and dividing by group std. Transform makes this easy because it broadcasts group stats back to each row.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Code["df['normalized'] = df.groupby('city')['sales']
.transform(lambda x: (x - x.mean()) / x.std())"] Code --> Steps["For each group:
1. Compute mean & std
2. Normalize each value
3. Broadcast back"] Code --> Result["Each row now has
z-score within its group"] Use["Use cases:
• Z-scores per group
• Percent of group total
• Fill NA with group mean"] style Code fill:#ADD8E6 style Use fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Filter: Keep/Remove Groups
steps.explanation: **filter()** keeps or removes entire groups based on a condition. The condition is evaluated per group (returns True/False). All rows in passing groups are kept; failing groups are dropped entirely.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Code["df.groupby('city')
.filter(lambda g: g['sales'].sum() > 300)"] subgraph "Groups" G1["NYC: sum=250
FAILS"] G2["LA: sum=500
PASSES"] end G1 --> Drop["Dropped"] G2 --> Keep["Kept (all rows)"] Result["Result: Only LA rows"] Keep --> Result style Drop fill:#FFB6C1 style Keep fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Apply: Full Flexibility
steps.explanation: **apply()** passes each group as a DataFrame to your function. Maximum flexibility - your function can return a scalar, Series, or DataFrame. But it's slower than built-in methods. Use only when necessary.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Apply["apply(custom_func)"] Apply --> Input["Each group passed
as DataFrame"] Input --> Func["Your function
can do anything"] Func --> Output["Return:
• Scalar → aggregation
• Series → new columns
• DataFrame → transform"] Warning["⚠️ Slower than built-ins
Use agg/transform when possible"] style Apply fill:#E6E6FA style Warning fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Grouping by Multiple Columns
steps.explanation: Pass a list to group by multiple columns. This creates a **MultiIndex** in the result. Use `as_index=False` to keep group columns as regular columns instead of index.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Code["df.groupby(['city', 'product']).sum()"] Code --> Multi["MultiIndex result:
(city, product) as index"] Alt["df.groupby(['city', 'product'],
as_index=False).sum()"] Alt --> Flat["Flat result:
city & product as columns"] Reset["Or: .reset_index()
after groupby"] style Code fill:#ADD8E6 style Alt fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: Putting it all together: group sales data by region, calculate multiple statistics, add normalized values, and filter to high-performing regions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "1. Basic Aggregation" A1["df.groupby('region')['sales'].sum()"] end subgraph "2. Multiple Stats" A2["df.groupby('region').agg(
total=('sales','sum'),
avg=('sales','mean'))"] end subgraph "3. Add Normalized Column" A3["df['pct'] = df.groupby('region')['sales']
.transform(lambda x: x/x.sum())"] end subgraph "4. Filter Groups" A4["df.groupby('region')
.filter(lambda g: len(g) >= 5)"] end style A1 fill:#ADD8E6 style A2 fill:#E6E6FA style A3 fill:#90EE90 style A4 fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: SQL Query Execution Order
fields.description: Understand the order SQL actually executes clauses: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT. Master this to write correct queries and debug issues.

steps.title: Written vs Execution Order
steps.explanation: SQL is **declarative** - you describe *what* you want, not *how* to get it. The order you write clauses differs from execution order. Understanding execution order helps you write correct queries and debug issues.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Written Order"
        W1["SELECT"]
        W2["FROM"]
        W3["WHERE"]
        W4["GROUP BY"]
        W5["HAVING"]
        W6["ORDER BY"]
        W1 --> W2 --> W3 --> W4 --> W5 --> W6
    end
    subgraph "Execution Order"
        E1["FROM"]
        E2["WHERE"]
        E3["GROUP BY"]
        E4["HAVING"]
        E5["SELECT"]
        E6["ORDER BY"]
        E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4 --> E5 --> E6
    end
    style W1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style E1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 1: FROM & JOIN
steps.explanation: Execution starts with **FROM** - identifying the source table(s). If there are **JOINs**, they're processed here too, combining tables into a working set. This creates the initial pool of rows to work with.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB FROM["1. FROM table_name"] FROM --> Source["Identify source table"] FROM --> Join["Process JOINs"] Join --> Inner["INNER JOIN"] Join --> Left["LEFT JOIN"] Join --> Right["RIGHT JOIN"] Join --> Cross["CROSS JOIN"] Result["Working set:
All rows from joined tables"] Inner --> Result Left --> Result style FROM fill:#90EE90 style Result fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Step 2: WHERE
steps.explanation: **WHERE** filters individual rows based on conditions. Only rows where the condition is TRUE pass through. This happens BEFORE grouping, so you **cannot use aggregate functions** (like SUM, COUNT) in WHERE.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB WHERE["2. WHERE condition"] Input["All rows from FROM"] Input --> WHERE WHERE --> Check{"Row meets
condition?"} Check --> |"TRUE"| Keep["Keep row"] Check --> |"FALSE"| Drop["Discard row"] Cannot["❌ Cannot use:
• Aggregate functions
• Column aliases from SELECT"] style WHERE fill:#90EE90 style Cannot fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Step 3: GROUP BY
steps.explanation: **GROUP BY** collects rows into groups based on column values. After this, you no longer have individual rows - you have groups. Any column in SELECT must be either in GROUP BY or wrapped in an aggregate function.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB GROUP["3. GROUP BY column"] subgraph "Before" R1["NYC | 100"] R2["LA | 200"] R3["NYC | 150"] R4["LA | 300"] end subgraph "After" G1["NYC group
(2 rows)"] G2["LA group
(2 rows)"] end R1 --> GROUP R2 --> GROUP R3 --> GROUP R4 --> GROUP GROUP --> G1 GROUP --> G2 Rule["SELECT must have:
• Grouped columns OR
• Aggregate functions"] style GROUP fill:#90EE90 style Rule fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Step 4: HAVING
steps.explanation: **HAVING** filters groups (not rows) based on aggregate conditions. It runs AFTER GROUP BY, so you CAN use aggregate functions here. Use WHERE for row conditions, HAVING for group conditions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB HAVING["4. HAVING condition"] Input["Groups from GROUP BY"] Input --> HAVING HAVING --> Check{"Group meets
condition?"} Check --> |"TRUE"| Keep["Keep group"] Check --> |"FALSE"| Drop["Discard group"] Example["HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
HAVING SUM(sales) > 1000"] Compare["WHERE = filter rows
HAVING = filter groups"] style HAVING fill:#90EE90 style Compare fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Step 5: SELECT
steps.explanation: **SELECT** chooses which columns to include in output. Expressions and aliases are evaluated here. This is why you can't use SELECT aliases in WHERE or GROUP BY - they don't exist yet! You CAN use them in ORDER BY.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB SELECT["5. SELECT columns"] SELECT --> Cols["Choose columns"] SELECT --> Expr["Evaluate expressions"] SELECT --> Alias["Create aliases"] SELECT --> Agg["Compute aggregates
(SUM, COUNT, etc.)"] Cannot["❌ Earlier clauses can't
see SELECT aliases"] Can["✓ ORDER BY CAN
use SELECT aliases"] style SELECT fill:#90EE90 style Cannot fill:#FFB6C1 style Can fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 6: DISTINCT
steps.explanation: **DISTINCT** removes duplicate rows from the result. It runs after SELECT, comparing entire rows (or specified columns). DISTINCT can be expensive on large datasets - it requires comparing all rows.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    DISTINCT["6. DISTINCT"]
    subgraph "Before"
        R1["NYC | Sales"]
        R2["LA | Marketing"]
        R3["NYC | Sales"]
    end
    subgraph "After"
        D1["NYC | Sales"]
        D2["LA | Marketing"]
    end
    R1 --> DISTINCT
    R2 --> DISTINCT
    R3 --> DISTINCT
    DISTINCT --> D1
    DISTINCT --> D2
    Note["Duplicate removed"]
    style DISTINCT fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 7: ORDER BY
steps.explanation: **ORDER BY** sorts the result set. It runs AFTER SELECT, so you CAN use column aliases and column positions (e.g., ORDER BY 1). Use ASC (default) or DESC. Multiple columns sort by first, then second, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB ORDER["7. ORDER BY"] ORDER --> Alias["Can use SELECT aliases!"] ORDER --> Pos["Can use column position
ORDER BY 1, 2"] ORDER --> Multi["Multiple columns:
ORDER BY city, sales DESC"] ORDER --> Dir["ASC (default) or DESC"] Runs["Runs after SELECT
so aliases exist"] style ORDER fill:#90EE90 style Runs fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Step 8: LIMIT & OFFSET
steps.explanation: **LIMIT** restricts how many rows to return. **OFFSET** skips rows before returning. These run last - after sorting. Essential for pagination. Syntax varies: MySQL uses LIMIT, SQL Server uses TOP.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB LIMIT["8. LIMIT / OFFSET"] LIMIT --> L["LIMIT 10
Return only 10 rows"] LIMIT --> O["OFFSET 20
Skip first 20 rows"] LIMIT --> Both["LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20
Rows 21-30"] Syntax["Syntax varies:
• MySQL: LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20
• PostgreSQL: LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20
• SQL Server: OFFSET 20 ROWS
FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY"] style LIMIT fill:#90EE90 style Syntax fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Complete Execution Flow
steps.explanation: Here's the full execution order with a visual pipeline. Data flows through each stage, getting filtered, grouped, and transformed until the final result.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB F["1. FROM/JOIN
Get all data"] W["2. WHERE
Filter rows"] G["3. GROUP BY
Make groups"] H["4. HAVING
Filter groups"] S["5. SELECT
Choose columns"] D["6. DISTINCT
Remove duplicates"] O["7. ORDER BY
Sort results"] L["8. LIMIT
Restrict output"] F --> W --> G --> H --> S --> D --> O --> L style F fill:#ADD8E6 style W fill:#ADD8E6 style G fill:#E6E6FA style H fill:#E6E6FA style S fill:#90EE90 style D fill:#90EE90 style O fill:#FFFACD style L fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Why Does This Matter?
steps.explanation: Understanding execution order explains common errors and behaviors: why aliases don't work in WHERE, why HAVING needs GROUP BY, why ORDER BY can use aliases but GROUP BY can't.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Matter["Why It Matters"] Matter --> E1["Can't use alias in WHERE
(SELECT hasn't run)"] Matter --> E2["Can't use aggregates in WHERE
(GROUP BY hasn't run)"] Matter --> E3["CAN use alias in ORDER BY
(SELECT has run)"] Matter --> E4["HAVING requires GROUP BY
(needs groups to filter)"] Correct["Understanding order
= writing correct SQL"] style Correct fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Example: Putting It Together
steps.explanation: Let's trace a real query through execution order. Watch how data transforms at each stage from raw rows to the final result.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Query["SELECT city, SUM(sales) as total
FROM orders
WHERE year = 2024
GROUP BY city
HAVING SUM(sales) > 1000
ORDER BY total DESC
LIMIT 5"] Query --> S1["1. FROM orders
→ all order rows"] S1 --> S2["2. WHERE year=2024
→ only 2024 orders"] S2 --> S3["3. GROUP BY city
→ rows grouped by city"] S3 --> S4["4. HAVING SUM>1000
→ big cities only"] S4 --> S5["5. SELECT city, SUM
→ compute totals"] S5 --> S6["6. ORDER BY total
→ sort descending"] S6 --> S7["7. LIMIT 5
→ top 5 cities"] style Query fill:#ADD8E6 style S7 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Common Gotchas
steps.explanation: Keep these execution order issues in mind: alias scope, aggregate placement, and NULL handling in WHERE vs HAVING.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Gotcha["Common Mistakes"] Gotcha --> G1["Using alias in WHERE
❌ WHERE total > 100
✓ WHERE SUM(x) > 100"] Gotcha --> G2["Aggregate in WHERE
❌ WHERE COUNT(*) > 5
✓ HAVING COUNT(*) > 5"] Gotcha --> G3["Non-grouped column in SELECT
❌ SELECT name, SUM(sales)
without GROUP BY name"] Gotcha --> G4["ORDER BY before GROUP BY
(logically impossible)"] style G1 fill:#FFB6C1 style G2 fill:#FFB6C1 style G3 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Memory Aid
steps.explanation: Remember the execution order with this mnemonic: **F**rom **W**here **G**roups **H**ave **S**elect **D**istinct **O**rdered **L**imits. Or just: 'Fetch Where Grouped, Having Selected Distinct Ordered Limits'.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Mnemonic["F W G H S D O L"] Mnemonic --> F["From"] F --> W["Where"] W --> G["Group by"] G --> H["Having"] H --> S["Select"] S --> D["Distinct"] D --> O["Order by"] O --> L["Limit"] Phrase["'Fetch Where Grouped,
Having Selected
Distinct Ordered Limits'"] style Phrase fill:#FFFACD style Mnemonic fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Encoder-Decoder Architecture
fields.description: Understand the complete Transformer architecture: encoder and decoder stacks, self-attention, cross-attention, masked attention, and how they work together for sequence-to-sequence tasks.

steps.title: The Big Picture
steps.explanation: The **Transformer** architecture revolutionized NLP. The original design has two parts: an **Encoder** that reads and understands input, and a **Decoder** that generates output. Introduced in 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017).
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Input["Input Sequence
'The cat sat'"] Input --> Encoder["ENCODER
Understands input"] Encoder --> Context["Encoded
Representation"] Context --> Decoder["DECODER
Generates output"] Decoder --> Output["Output Sequence
'Le chat assis'"] style Encoder fill:#ADD8E6 style Decoder fill:#E6E6FA style Context fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Encoder-Decoder Overview
steps.explanation: The encoder processes the entire input at once (bidirectional). The decoder generates output one token at a time (autoregressive), attending to both its own output and the encoder's representation. They're connected via **cross-attention**.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Encoder (Nx layers)" E1["Self-Attention
(sees all input)"] E2["Feed Forward"] E1 --> E2 end subgraph "Decoder (Nx layers)" D1["Masked Self-Attention
(sees only past output)"] D2["Cross-Attention
(sees encoder output)"] D3["Feed Forward"] D1 --> D2 --> D3 end E2 --> |"Keys, Values"| D2 style E1 fill:#ADD8E6 style D2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Input: Embeddings + Position
steps.explanation: Input tokens are converted to **embeddings** (dense vectors). Since transformers process all tokens in parallel (no recurrence), we add **positional encodings** so the model knows token order. These are added together.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Tokens["Input Tokens
[The, cat, sat]"] Tokens --> Embed["Token Embeddings
(learned vectors)"] Pos["Positional Encoding
(position info)"] Embed --> Add["Add Together"] Pos --> Add Add --> Input["Input to Encoder"] Formula["PE uses sin/cos functions
or learned embeddings"] style Add fill:#90EE90 style Formula fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Encoder Block
steps.explanation: Each encoder layer has two sub-layers: **Multi-Head Self-Attention** and **Feed-Forward Network**. Each sub-layer has a **residual connection** (add input to output) and **layer normalization**. Original paper stacks 6 identical layers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Input["Input"] Input --> Attn["Multi-Head
Self-Attention"] Attn --> Add1["Add & Norm"] Input --> Add1 Add1 --> FF["Feed-Forward
Network"] FF --> Add2["Add & Norm"] Add1 --> Add2 Add2 --> Output["To Next Layer"] Residual["Residual connections
help gradient flow"] style Attn fill:#ADD8E6 style FF fill:#E6E6FA style Residual fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Multi-Head Self-Attention
steps.explanation: Self-attention lets each token attend to all other tokens. **Multi-head** means we do this multiple times in parallel with different learned projections, then concatenate. This captures different types of relationships.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Input["Input"] Input --> H1["Head 1"] Input --> H2["Head 2"] Input --> H3["Head 3"] Input --> Hn["Head N"] H1 --> Concat["Concatenate"] H2 --> Concat H3 --> Concat Hn --> Concat Concat --> Linear["Linear Projection"] Linear --> Output["Output"] Heads["Each head learns
different attention patterns
(syntax, semantics, etc.)"] style Concat fill:#90EE90 style Heads fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Attention: Q, K, V
steps.explanation: Each attention head creates **Query (Q)**, **Key (K)**, and **Value (V)** from input. Attention scores = softmax(QK^T / √d). Output = weighted sum of Values. Think: Q asks, K answers how relevant, V provides the content.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Input["Input X"] Input --> Q["Q = X·Wq
(Query)"] Input --> K["K = X·Wk
(Key)"] Input --> V["V = X·Wv
(Value)"] Q --> Score["Score = Q·K^T / √d"] K --> Score Score --> Soft["Softmax
(attention weights)"] Soft --> Out["Output = weights · V"] V --> Out style Score fill:#ADD8E6 style Out fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Feed-Forward Network
steps.explanation: After attention, each position passes through a **feed-forward network** (FFN) independently. It's two linear transformations with ReLU/GELU activation: FFN(x) = W₂·ReLU(W₁·x + b₁) + b₂. Same weights for all positions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Input["Input
(d_model)"] Input --> L1["Linear 1
(d_model → d_ff)"] L1 --> Act["ReLU/GELU"] Act --> L2["Linear 2
(d_ff → d_model)"] L2 --> Output["Output
(d_model)"] Note["d_ff typically 4x d_model
(e.g., 512 → 2048)"] style Act fill:#E6E6FA style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Decoder Block
steps.explanation: The decoder has THREE sub-layers: **Masked Self-Attention** (sees only past outputs), **Cross-Attention** (attends to encoder), and **Feed-Forward**. Each has residual connection + layer norm.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Input["Previous Output"] Input --> Mask["Masked
Self-Attention"] Mask --> Add1["Add & Norm"] Input --> Add1 Add1 --> Cross["Cross-Attention
(to Encoder)"] Enc["Encoder Output"] --> Cross Cross --> Add2["Add & Norm"] Add1 --> Add2 Add2 --> FF["Feed-Forward"] FF --> Add3["Add & Norm"] Add2 --> Add3 Add3 --> Output["To Next Layer"] style Mask fill:#FFB6C1 style Cross fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Masked Self-Attention
steps.explanation: In the decoder, self-attention must be **masked** to prevent looking at future tokens. During training, we process all positions at once but mask future positions. This maintains the autoregressive property.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Attention Matrix (Masked)" M["Position 1 sees: [1, -, -, -]
Position 2 sees: [1, 2, -, -]
Position 3 sees: [1, 2, 3, -]
Position 4 sees: [1, 2, 3, 4]"] end Why["Why mask?
• During generation, future doesn't exist
• Training must match inference
• Prevents 'cheating'"] Mask["Mask = -∞ before softmax
→ zero attention weight"] style M fill:#ADD8E6 style Why fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Cross-Attention
steps.explanation: **Cross-attention** connects decoder to encoder. Queries come from the decoder, but Keys and Values come from the encoder. This lets the decoder 'look at' the input while generating each output token.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Dec["Decoder State"] Enc["Encoder Output"] Dec --> Q["Query
(from decoder)"] Enc --> K["Key
(from encoder)"] Enc --> V["Value
(from encoder)"] Q --> Attn["Attention"] K --> Attn V --> Attn Attn --> Out["Decoder attends
to relevant input"] Key["This is how decoder
'reads' the input!"] style Attn fill:#90EE90 style Key fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Output Layer
steps.explanation: The decoder's final output passes through a **linear layer** projecting to vocabulary size, then **softmax** to get probabilities. The highest probability token is selected (or sampled). This repeats until end token.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Dec["Decoder Output
(d_model)"] Dec --> Linear["Linear Layer
(d_model → vocab_size)"] Linear --> Soft["Softmax"] Soft --> Probs["Probabilities
over vocabulary"] Probs --> Select["Select Token
(argmax or sample)"] Select --> Token["Output: 'chat'"] Token --> |"Feed back"| Dec style Soft fill:#90EE90 style Select fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Training vs Inference
steps.explanation: **Training**: Process all outputs in parallel using teacher forcing (feed ground truth). **Inference**: Generate one token at a time, feeding each output back as input. Masking ensures training matches inference behavior.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Training (Parallel)"
        T1["Input: [start, Le, chat]"]
        T2["Target: [Le, chat, end]"]
        T3["Process all at once"]
        T4["Teacher forcing"]
        T1 --> T3
        T2 --> T3
    end
    subgraph "Inference (Sequential)"
        I1["Generate 'Le'"]
        I2["Feed back, generate 'chat'"]
        I3["Feed back, generate 'end'"]
        I4["Stop"]
        I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4
    end
    style T3 fill:#ADD8E6
    style I4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Architecture Variants
steps.explanation: Different tasks use different parts: **Encoder-only** (BERT) for understanding, **Decoder-only** (GPT) for generation, **Encoder-Decoder** (T5, BART) for seq2seq tasks like translation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Encoder-Only" EO["BERT, RoBERTa"] EO --> EOU["Classification
NER, QA"] end subgraph "Decoder-Only" DO["GPT, LLaMA, Claude"] DO --> DOU["Text Generation
Completion"] end subgraph "Encoder-Decoder" ED["T5, BART, mT5"] ED --> EDU["Translation
Summarization"] end style EO fill:#ADD8E6 style DO fill:#E6E6FA style ED fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Complete Architecture
steps.explanation: Putting it all together: Input embeddings + positional encoding → N encoder layers → N decoder layers (with cross-attention) → Linear + Softmax → Output tokens. Original paper: N=6, d_model=512, 8 heads.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB In["Input Tokens"] In --> InEmb["Embed + Position"] InEmb --> E1["Encoder Layer 1"] E1 --> E2["..."] E2 --> EN["Encoder Layer N"] Out["Output Tokens
(shifted right)"] Out --> OutEmb["Embed + Position"] OutEmb --> D1["Decoder Layer 1"] D1 --> D2["..."] D2 --> DN["Decoder Layer N"] EN --> |"K, V"| D1 EN --> |"K, V"| D2 EN --> |"K, V"| DN DN --> Linear["Linear + Softmax"] Linear --> Pred["Next Token"] style EN fill:#ADD8E6 style DN fill:#E6E6FA style Pred fill:#90EE90

fields.title: SQL JOIN Types Visualized
fields.description: Master all SQL JOIN types: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL OUTER, CROSS, and Self JOINs. Understand when to use each with visual Venn diagrams and practical examples.

steps.title: What Are JOINs?
steps.explanation: **JOINs** combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column. They're fundamental to relational databases - data is split across tables, and JOINs bring it back together. The type of JOIN determines which rows are included.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Table A" A1["Users"] end subgraph "Table B" B1["Orders"] end A1 --> Join["JOIN ON
users.id = orders.user_id"] B1 --> Join Join --> Result["Combined
Result"] style Join fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Sample Tables
steps.explanation: Let's use two simple tables for all examples: **Users** (id, name) and **Orders** (id, user_id, product). Notice: User 3 has no orders, and Order 103 has no matching user (user_id=99).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Users Table" U["id | name
───────────
1 | Alice
2 | Bob
3 | Carol"] end subgraph "Orders Table" O["id | user_id | product
─────────────────────────
101 | 1 | Laptop
102 | 2 | Phone
103 | 99 | Tablet"] end Note["User 3 (Carol): no orders
Order 103: no matching user"] style U fill:#ADD8E6 style O fill:#E6E6FA style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: INNER JOIN
steps.explanation: **INNER JOIN** returns only rows where there's a match in BOTH tables. No match = not included. This is the most common JOIN type. Think of it as the intersection.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Venn Diagram" V["(A) ◉ (B)
Only the overlap"] end Query["SELECT *
FROM users u
INNER JOIN orders o
ON u.id = o.user_id"] subgraph "Result" R["Alice | Laptop
Bob | Phone"] end Query --> R Missing["❌ Carol (no orders)
❌ Order 103 (no user)"] style V fill:#90EE90 style Missing fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: LEFT JOIN
steps.explanation: **LEFT JOIN** (or LEFT OUTER JOIN) returns ALL rows from the left table, plus matching rows from the right. No match on right = NULL values. Preserves the 'left' table completely.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Venn Diagram" V["(A●●●) ◉ (B)
All of A + overlap"] end Query["SELECT *
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o
ON u.id = o.user_id"] subgraph "Result" R["Alice | Laptop
Bob | Phone
Carol | NULL"] end Query --> R Note["✓ Carol included
(with NULL order)
❌ Order 103 excluded"] style V fill:#ADD8E6 style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: RIGHT JOIN
steps.explanation: **RIGHT JOIN** (or RIGHT OUTER JOIN) returns ALL rows from the right table, plus matching rows from the left. Mirror of LEFT JOIN. In practice, most people just reorder tables and use LEFT JOIN instead.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Venn Diagram" V["(A) ◉ (●●●B)
Overlap + all of B"] end Query["SELECT *
FROM users u
RIGHT JOIN orders o
ON u.id = o.user_id"] subgraph "Result" R["Alice | Laptop
Bob | Phone
NULL | Tablet"] end Query --> R Note["❌ Carol excluded
✓ Order 103 included
(with NULL user)"] style V fill:#E6E6FA style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: FULL OUTER JOIN
steps.explanation: **FULL OUTER JOIN** returns ALL rows from BOTH tables. Matches where possible, NULLs where not. Think of it as the union of LEFT and RIGHT JOINs. Not supported in MySQL (use UNION instead).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Venn Diagram" V["(●●●A●●●) ◉ (●●●B●●●)
Everything from both"] end Query["SELECT *
FROM users u
FULL OUTER JOIN orders o
ON u.id = o.user_id"] subgraph "Result" R["Alice | Laptop
Bob | Phone
Carol | NULL
NULL | Tablet"] end Query --> R Note["✓ All users
✓ All orders
NULLs fill gaps"] style V fill:#90EE90 style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: CROSS JOIN
steps.explanation: **CROSS JOIN** returns the Cartesian product - every row from A paired with every row from B. No ON condition needed. Result size = rows(A) × rows(B). Rarely used intentionally but useful for generating combinations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Query["SELECT *
FROM users
CROSS JOIN orders"] subgraph "Result (3 × 3 = 9 rows)" R["Alice | Laptop
Alice | Phone
Alice | Tablet
Bob | Laptop
Bob | Phone
Bob | Tablet
Carol | Laptop
Carol | Phone
Carol | Tablet"] end Query --> R Warning["⚠️ Can explode!
1000 × 1000 = 1,000,000 rows"] style Warning fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Self JOIN
steps.explanation: A **Self JOIN** joins a table to itself. Useful for hierarchical data (employees → managers) or comparing rows within the same table. Must use table aliases to distinguish the two 'copies'.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Employees Table" E["id | name | manager_id
───────────────────────
1 | Alice | NULL
2 | Bob | 1
3 | Carol | 1"] end Query["SELECT e.name, m.name as manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m
ON e.manager_id = m.id"] subgraph "Result" R["Bob | Alice
Carol | Alice
Alice | NULL"] end Query --> R style E fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: JOIN Conditions: ON vs WHERE
steps.explanation: **ON** specifies how tables relate (the join condition). **WHERE** filters the combined result. For INNER JOIN, they're equivalent. For OUTER JOINs, WHERE filters AFTER joining and can eliminate unmatched rows!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "LEFT JOIN with ON" Q1["LEFT JOIN orders o
ON u.id = o.user_id
AND o.product = 'Laptop'"] R1["Alice | Laptop
Bob | NULL
Carol | NULL"] end subgraph "LEFT JOIN with WHERE" Q2["LEFT JOIN orders o
ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE o.product = 'Laptop'"] R2["Alice | Laptop"] end Diff["ON: Filters during join
WHERE: Filters after join"] style R1 fill:#90EE90 style R2 fill:#FFB6C1 style Diff fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Multiple JOINs
steps.explanation: You can chain multiple JOINs to combine many tables. Each JOIN connects to the growing result set. Order can matter for readability and sometimes performance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Query["SELECT u.name, o.id, p.name
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
JOIN products p ON o.product_id = p.id
JOIN categories c ON p.cat_id = c.id"] Flow["users → orders → products → categories"] Tip["Tip: Start with your
'main' table, then join
related tables in sequence"] style Query fill:#ADD8E6 style Tip fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Finding Non-Matches
steps.explanation: A powerful pattern: LEFT JOIN + WHERE right.column IS NULL finds rows in the left table that have NO match in the right table. Great for finding orphans or missing data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Query["SELECT u.*
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o
ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE o.id IS NULL"] subgraph "Result" R["Carol
(no orders)"] end Query --> R Pattern["Pattern: Find A without B
LEFT JOIN B ON ...
WHERE B.key IS NULL"] Uses["Use cases:
• Users without orders
• Products never sold
• Orphaned records"] style Pattern fill:#90EE90 style Uses fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Performance Tips
steps.explanation: JOIN performance depends on indexes, table size, and join type. Always index your join columns. Be careful with CROSS JOINs. Consider join order for large queries.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Tips["Performance Tips"] Tips --> T1["Index join columns
(foreign keys)"] Tips --> T2["Join smaller tables first
(if possible)"] Tips --> T3["Avoid CROSS JOIN
unless needed"] Tips --> T4["Filter early with WHERE
(reduce rows before join)"] Tips --> T5["Use EXPLAIN to
analyze join plans"] style Tips fill:#ADD8E6 style T1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Choosing the Right JOIN
steps.explanation: Pick your JOIN based on what data you need: matching rows only? all from one side? all from both? Here's a decision guide.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Q["What do you need?"] Q --> Q1{"Only matching
rows?"} Q1 --> |"Yes"| Inner["INNER JOIN"] Q1 --> |"No"| Q2{"All from
left table?"} Q2 --> |"Yes"| Left["LEFT JOIN"] Q2 --> |"No"| Q3{"All from
both tables?"} Q3 --> |"Yes"| Full["FULL OUTER JOIN"] Q3 --> |"No"| Q4{"Every
combination?"} Q4 --> |"Yes"| Cross["CROSS JOIN"] style Inner fill:#90EE90 style Left fill:#ADD8E6 style Full fill:#E6E6FA style Cross fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Summary of all JOIN types with visual representation. Keep this mental model: which parts of the Venn diagram do you want to include?
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "JOIN Types Summary" I["INNER: A ∩ B
Only matches"] L["LEFT: A + (A ∩ B)
All A, matching B"] R["RIGHT: (A ∩ B) + B
Matching A, all B"] F["FULL: A ∪ B
Everything"] C["CROSS: A × B
All combinations"] end I --> IM["Most common"] L --> LM["Second most common"] F --> FM["Not in MySQL"] C --> CM["Use carefully!"] style I fill:#90EE90 style L fill:#ADD8E6 style F fill:#E6E6FA style C fill:#FFB6C1

fields.title: MLflow Tracking: Runs, Experiments & Parameters
fields.description: Master MLflow's core tracking concepts: experiments, runs, parameters, metrics, and artifacts. Essential for ML interview questions about experiment management.

steps.title: MLflow Tracking Overview
steps.explanation: **MLflow Tracking** is the core component for logging and querying ML experiments. It organizes work into **Experiments** (containers) and **Runs** (individual executions). Each run can log **parameters**, **metrics**, and **artifacts**.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB MLflow["MLflow Tracking"] MLflow --> Exp["Experiments
Group related runs"] MLflow --> Runs["Runs
Single execution"] MLflow --> Storage["Tracking Server
Store & query"] Runs --> Params["Parameters"] Runs --> Metrics["Metrics"] Runs --> Artifacts["Artifacts"] style MLflow fill:#ADD8E6 style Exp fill:#90EE90 style Runs fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Experiments: Organizing Your Work
steps.explanation: An **Experiment** is a named container for runs. Think of it as a folder for a specific project or model type. Each experiment has a unique ID and can have multiple runs. Use `mlflow.set_experiment('name')` to create or select one.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Experiment: fraud-detection" R1["Run 1: Baseline RF"] R2["Run 2: XGBoost"] R3["Run 3: Neural Net"] end subgraph "Experiment: churn-prediction" R4["Run 1: Logistic Reg"] R5["Run 2: LightGBM"] end Code["mlflow.set_experiment
('fraud-detection')"] Code --> R1 style Code fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Starting a Run
steps.explanation: A **Run** represents a single execution of your ML code. Use `mlflow.start_run()` as a context manager. Each run gets a unique **run_id** and timestamp. Runs track everything: code version, parameters, metrics, and outputs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Python Code" Start["with mlflow.start_run():"] Body[" # Your ML code
# Log params/metrics
# Train model"] End["# Run auto-ends"] Start --> Body --> End end Start --> RunCreated["Run Created
run_id: abc123
status: RUNNING"] End --> RunEnded["Run Ended
status: FINISHED"] style Start fill:#90EE90 style RunCreated fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Logging Parameters
steps.explanation: **Parameters** are input settings that don't change during training: hyperparameters, data paths, feature flags. Log with `mlflow.log_param('name', value)` or `mlflow.log_params(dict)`. Parameters are strings - numbers get converted.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "What to Log as Parameters" P1["learning_rate = 0.01"] P2["n_estimators = 100"] P3["max_depth = 5"] P4["data_version = 'v2.1'"] end Code["mlflow.log_params({
'learning_rate': 0.01,
'n_estimators': 100,
'max_depth': 5
})"] P1 --> Code P2 --> Code P3 --> Code Note["Tip: Log everything
needed to reproduce"] style Code fill:#ADD8E6 style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Logging Metrics
steps.explanation: **Metrics** are numeric values that measure model performance. They can be logged once or as a series over time (e.g., loss per epoch). Use `mlflow.log_metric('name', value, step=N)` for tracking progress during training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Single Metrics" M1["accuracy = 0.95"] M2["f1_score = 0.87"] M3["auc_roc = 0.92"] end subgraph "Step Metrics (Training)" S1["loss @ step 0 = 2.5"] S2["loss @ step 1 = 1.8"] S3["loss @ step 2 = 1.2"] end Code1["mlflow.log_metric
('accuracy', 0.95)"] Code2["mlflow.log_metric
('loss', 1.8, step=1)"] M1 --> Code1 S2 --> Code2 style Code1 fill:#90EE90 style Code2 fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Logging Artifacts
steps.explanation: **Artifacts** are output files: trained models, plots, data samples, configs. Use `mlflow.log_artifact('path')` for single files or `mlflow.log_artifacts('dir')` for directories. MLflow copies files to the artifact store.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Common Artifacts" A1["model.pkl"] A2["confusion_matrix.png"] A3["feature_importance.csv"] A4["config.yaml"] end Log["mlflow.log_artifact
('model.pkl')"] A1 --> Log Log --> Store["Artifact Store
(S3/GCS/local)"] Query["Later: Download via
mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts()"] Store --> Query style Log fill:#ADD8E6 style Store fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Comparing Runs in the UI
steps.explanation: The **MLflow UI** (`mlflow ui`) lets you compare runs visually. Filter by experiment, sort by metrics, view parameter differences. Select multiple runs to see side-by-side comparisons. Essential for hyperparameter tuning analysis.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB UI["MLflow UI
localhost:5000"] UI --> List["Run List View
Sort by accuracy ↓"] UI --> Compare["Compare View
Side-by-side params"] UI --> Charts["Charts View
Metric over time"] List --> Select["Select runs
to compare"] Select --> Compare Cmd["$ mlflow ui"] Cmd --> UI style UI fill:#ADD8E6 style Cmd fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Complete Tracking Example
steps.explanation: Here's a complete example combining all concepts. The pattern is: set experiment → start run → log params → train → log metrics → log artifacts. This creates a fully reproducible experiment record.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Complete Pattern" S1["1. mlflow.set_experiment('my-project')"] S2["2. with mlflow.start_run():"] S3["3. mlflow.log_params(config)"] S4["4. model = train(X, y)"] S5["5. mlflow.log_metrics(scores)"] S6["6. mlflow.log_artifact(model_path)"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 end S6 --> Result["Reproducible Run
with full lineage"] style S1 fill:#E6E6FA style S2 fill:#ADD8E6 style S3 fill:#FFFACD style S5 fill:#90EE90 style Result fill:#90EE90

fields.title: MLflow Model Registry Lifecycle
fields.description: Understand the MLflow Model Registry: model versioning, stage transitions (Staging → Production → Archived), and deployment workflows. Critical for production ML interviews.

steps.title: What is the Model Registry?
steps.explanation: The **Model Registry** is MLflow's central hub for managing model versions and their lifecycle. It provides **versioning**, **stage transitions**, **annotations**, and **lineage tracking**. Think of it as Git for ML models.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Registry["MLflow Model Registry"] Registry --> Version["Versioning
v1, v2, v3..."] Registry --> Stages["Stage Management
Staging → Production"] Registry --> Meta["Metadata
Description, tags"] Registry --> Lineage["Lineage
Which run created this?"] style Registry fill:#ADD8E6 style Stages fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Registering a Model
steps.explanation: Register models with `mlflow.register_model()` or via the UI. You provide a **model URI** (from a run) and a **name**. First registration creates the model, subsequent ones create new **versions** under the same name.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Run["MLflow Run
with logged model"] Run --> URI["Model URI
runs:/abc123/model"] URI --> Register["mlflow.register_model
(uri, 'fraud-detector')"] Register --> V1["fraud-detector
Version 1"] Run2["Another Run"] --> Register Register --> V2["fraud-detector
Version 2"] style Register fill:#ADD8E6 style V1 fill:#90EE90 style V2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Model Stages Explained
steps.explanation: Each model version has a **stage**: **None** (just registered), **Staging** (testing/validation), **Production** (serving live traffic), or **Archived** (deprecated). Stages enable controlled promotion workflows.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR None["None
Just registered"] Staging["Staging
Testing & validation"] Prod["Production
Serving traffic"] Archived["Archived
Deprecated"] None --> |"promote"| Staging Staging --> |"promote"| Prod Prod --> |"archive"| Archived Staging --> |"reject"| Archived style None fill:#E6E6FA style Staging fill:#FFFACD style Prod fill:#90EE90 style Archived fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Transitioning Stages
steps.explanation: Use `MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage()` to move models between stages. You can **archive existing** production models automatically when promoting a new one. This ensures only one production version.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Promotion Flow" V2["Version 2
in Staging"] Transition["client.transition_model_version_stage
('fraud-detector', 2,
'Production',
archive_existing=True)"] V2 --> Transition end Transition --> Result1["Version 2
→ Production"] Transition --> Result2["Version 1
→ Archived"] style Transition fill:#ADD8E6 style Result1 fill:#90EE90 style Result2 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Loading Models by Stage
steps.explanation: Load models using the **models:/** URI scheme with stage or version. `models:/name/Production` always gets the current production model. `models:/name/1` gets a specific version. This decouples deployment from version numbers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Loading Options" L1["models:/fraud-detector/Production
→ Current production model"] L2["models:/fraud-detector/Staging
→ Current staging model"] L3["models:/fraud-detector/3
→ Specific version 3"] L4["models:/fraud-detector/latest
→ Latest version (any stage)"] end Code["model = mlflow.pyfunc.load_model
('models:/fraud-detector/Production')"] L1 --> Code style L1 fill:#90EE90 style L2 fill:#FFFACD style Code fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Model Lineage & Metadata
steps.explanation: The registry tracks **lineage**: which run created each version, what parameters were used, training metrics. Add **descriptions** and **tags** for documentation. This enables auditing and debugging production issues.

steps.explanation: A typical workflow: (1) Train and log model in a run, (2) Register model, (3) Test in Staging, (4) Promote to Production, (5) Deployment system loads from registry. The registry becomes the **single source of truth**.

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: **Best practices**: (1) Use meaningful model names, (2) Always add descriptions, (3) Tag with team/project, (4) Never skip Staging, (5) Archive don't delete, (6) Use `archive_existing` for atomic promotions. This ensures safe, traceable deployments.
